v.t: Trajan launches the Second Dacian War
v.s: Decebalus, king of Dacia, despite submitting after the First Dacian War, again violated the treaty, expanded his army, and raided Roman borders. Emperor Trajan of Rome personally led a large army across the Danube, employing a pincer movement deep into Dacian territory. After fierce fighting, the Romans captured the Dacian capital Sarmizegetusa Regia. Decebalus fled to the mountains to wage guerrilla warfare. This battle laid the foundation for Rome's total conquest of Dacia, and the immense gold looted filled Rome's treasury, funding subsequent large-scale public construction.

v.t: Ban Chao leads campaign to pacify Karasahr
v.s: Ban Chao, the Protector General of the Western Regions of the Eastern Han, in order to eliminate the remnants of the Northern Xiongnu and the treacherous kingdoms of Karasahr and Quli, allied with other Western Regions states such as Kucha and Shanshan to form a coalition. The Han army split into two forces: one led by Ban Chao's main force made a surprise attack on Karasahr via a hidden path, while the other cut off their retreat. King Yuan Meng of Karasahr was forced to surrender. Ban Chao executed him and installed a new king. After this battle, all fifty-plus states of the Western Regions submitted to the Eastern Han, the southern Silk Road was completely opened, and Ban Chao was enfeoffed as Marquis Dingyuan for his achievements.

v.t: Ban Chao dispatches Gan Ying on a mission to Daqin (Roman Empire)
v.s: To establish direct diplomatic and trade links with the Roman Empire, Ban Chao sent his subordinate Gan Ying at the head of a delegation from the Western Regions along the northern Silk Road. The delegation passed through Parthia and reached the coast of the Persian Gulf, preparing to sail to Daqin (the Roman Empire). However, Parthian merchants, seeking to monopolize Silk Road trade, deliberately exaggerated the perils of the sea voyage, claiming it would take three years and that sailors would easily contract sea fevers. Heeding these words, Gan Ying abandoned the sea crossing and returned. Although he did not reach Rome, Gan Ying brought back detailed geographical knowledge of Central and West Asia, recorded in the Book of the Later Han, deepening the Han dynasty's understanding of the distant West.

v.t: Trajan holds a magnificent triumph in Rome
v.s: After his decisive victory in the Second Dacian War, Emperor Trajan returned to Rome and held a grand triumph lasting 123 days. The triumphal procession displayed the captured treasury of King Decebalus of Dacia, vast quantities of weapons, prisoners of war, and reliefs depicting scenes from the war. The Senate awarded Trajan the title 'Dacicus' (Conqueror of Dacia) and declared the anniversary of the Dacian War a public holiday. The triumph also distributed huge sums of money and grain to Roman citizens and featured spectacular gladiatorial games, greatly boosting imperial morale.

v.t: The Library of Alexandria hosts a large academic debate
v.s: Under Roman rule, the Library of Alexandria remained the Mediterranean's leading academic center. That year, a public debate was held on whether the universe was geocentric or heliocentric. Scholars from Greek, Jewish, and Egyptian backgrounds clashed vigorously using mathematics and philosophy. Although the geocentric view won, the debate prompted scholars like Ptolemy to further observe astronomical data. The event also organized the library's 700,000 papyrus scrolls, attracting many scholars to study mathematics, medicine, and astronomy, continuing the scholarly prosperity from the Ptolemaic era.

v.t: The Parthian Empire and Roman Empire engage in prolonged military standoff in Mesopotamia
v.s: As Rome expanded eastward, the Parthian Empire felt directly threatened. The two powers engaged in a decades-long tug-of-war over control of the Kingdom of Armenia and the region of Mesopotamia. King Pacorus II of Parthia massed troops along the border, building a series of fortresses; Rome established the province of Mesopotamia and stationed two legions. Although no large-scale war broke out, skirmishes and proxy wars were frequent, often interrupting Silk Road trade. This tense standoff lasted half a century, severely draining both empires and paving the way for the rise of the Sassanian Persians.

v.t: Eastern Han and Southern Xiongnu restart border markets
v.s: To ease border tensions and secure strategic resources like horses, cattle, and sheep, the Eastern Han court reopened border markets with the Southern Xiongnu in commanderies such as Bingzhou and Liangzhou. Chinese merchants exchanged silk, ironware, grain, and tea for Xiongnu livestock and furs. The markets, supervised by local officials with a 3% tax rate, saw annual trade worth hundreds of millions of coins. This not only improved relations but also provided stable income for border residents, and tens of thousands of Xiongnu settled along the border engaging in trade and farming, accelerating ethnic integration.

v.t: Eastern Han implements frontier military-agricultural colonies (Tuntian)
v.s: To relieve the heavy burden of transporting grain to the frontiers and defend against Qiang and Xianbei incursions, the Eastern Han court, adopting the advice of Minister Yu Xu, extensively established military-agricultural colonies in border regions like Liangzhou, Bingzhou, and Shuofang. The court recruited landless peasants and convicts from the interior, granting each family 50 mu of land, oxen, and seeds, with a three-year tax holiday followed by a 30% harvest tax for military grain. The colonist-soldiers farmed and trained during off-seasons. Within a decade, the colonies cultivated millions of mu, producing enough grain to meet most garrison needs, significantly strengthening Han border defenses.

v.t: Ban Chao, Protector General of the Western Regions, returns to Luoyang and dies of illness
v.s: After 31 years in the Western Regions, Ban Chao, old and ill, petitioned to retire to his hometown. Emperor He of Han granted permission. His sister Ban Zhao personally greeted him at the city gate; the siblings wept with emotion. Only a month after returning to Luoyang, Ban Chao died of old war wounds and travel fatigue at age 71. The court posthumously honored him and bestowed the title Marquis Dingyuan. Ban Chao’s courage ('How can one catch tiger cubs without entering the tiger's lair?') in recovering the Western Regions and ensuring Silk Road peace for over 30 years became the most brilliant chapter in Eastern Han diplomacy.

v.t: Gandharan Buddhist art reaches its peak in the Kushan Empire
v.s: Under the patronage of Emperor Kanishka I of the Kushan Empire, Buddhist art in the Gandhara region (present-day northern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan) entered a golden age. Artisans blended Hellenistic realism with Indian religious symbolism to create the first anthropomorphic statues of the Buddha and Bodhisattvas. Representative works include a colossal standing Buddha about 15 meters tall, draped in a Greek-style robe with distinctly European facial features. Gandharan art later spread to China along the Silk Road, profoundly influencing the cave temple sculptures at Yungang and Longmen.

v.t: Trajan reforms the Roman Senate
v.s: To strengthen centralization and improve administrative efficiency, Emperor Trajan reformed the long-ineffective Senate. He greatly increased the number of seats for wealthy merchants, military nobles, and jurists from Italy and the provinces, strictly setting a property qualification of 1 million sesterces for senators. He also established a financial oversight mechanism, auditing Senate expenditures monthly and forbidding senators from engaging in commerce. After reform, the Senate was stabilized at 600 members, efficiency improved, and corruption declined, laying the institutional foundation for the prosperity of the 'Five Good Emperors' era.

v.t: Emperor He of Han tours Jingzhou to inspect water conservancy projects
v.s: Emperor He, accompanied by Empress Deng Sui and officials, toured Jingzhou, focusing on the embankment and irrigation works along the Yangtze and Han Rivers. Jingzhou had suffered frequent floods; the court had allocated 5 million coins to build a 300-li long flood control dike and dredge numerous canals. The emperor personally ascended the dike, offered wine and meat to the laborers, exempted Jingzhou from all taxes for the year, and selected twenty filial and incorrupt candidates for the Imperial University. This tour eased court-local tensions and boosted southern economic development.

v.t: Construction begins on Trajan's Forum in Rome
v.s: Using the enormous spoils from the Dacian Wars, Emperor Trajan initiated a grand architectural complex in Rome – Trajan's Forum. Measuring 300 meters long and 185 meters wide, it was four times larger than the earlier Forum of Augustus. At its center stood a gilded equestrian statue of Trajan; behind it rose the 38-meter-high Trajan's Column, its spiral relief 200 meters long depicting 155 scenes from the Dacian Wars. On either side were two symmetrical libraries (Greek and Latin) and a large market hall. The project consumed tens of thousands of tons of Carrara marble, employed over 10,000 craftsmen and slaves, and took five years to complete, becoming one of the most magnificent public complexes of the ancient world, symbolizing Roman power and Trajan's martial glory.

v.t: Wuhuan and Xianbei tribes submit en masse to Eastern Han
v.s: Influenced by the Southern Xiongnu and the Han's strong frontier policies, the leaders of Wuhuan and Xianbei tribes who had long roamed the Liaoxi and Youbeiping areas led over 20,000 tribespeople and 100,000 livestock to surrender to the Han's Liaodong Dependent State. They offered bows and warhorses, requesting settlement and Han titles. Emperor He approved their submission, settling them in border commanderies like Liaodong and Liaoxi, establishing dependent states, enfeoffing their leaders as kings and marquises, and granting gold seals, silk, and grain. This secured decades of peace on the northern border, as the submitted tribes became an important buffer against the Northern Xiongnu and other Xianbei.

v.t: Germanic tribes in Northern Europe accelerate social stratification and migration
v.s: Due to climate change and population pressure, Germanic tribes (such as Goths, Vandals, Burgundians) living in Scandinavia and along the Baltic Sea began to experience clear social stratification. Military leaders and priests gradually replaced ancient tribal assemblies, forming hereditary aristocracies. Armed conflicts intensified between tribes over better pastures and spoils, forcing the defeated to migrate southeast toward the East European Plain. This migration wave lasted nearly 200 years, eventually leading to large-scale Germanic invasions of the Roman Empire from the 3rd century onward, marking the beginning of the 'Barbarian Invasions'.

v.t: The Nyaya school of Indian philosophy takes initial shape
v.s: On the Indian subcontinent, the philosopher Gotama systematically compiled ancient logic and debate techniques, composing the foundational chapters of the Nyaya Sutras. The Nyaya school established sixteen categories (pramana, prameya, samshaya, prayojana, drishtanta, siddhanta, avayava, tarka, nirnaya, vada, jalpa, vitanda, hetvabhasa, chala, jati, nigrahasthana), emphasizing logical reasoning and debate as means to valid knowledge. This school, along with Vaisheshika, Samkhya, and others, formed the 'six orthodox philosophies,' greatly advancing Indian logic, epistemology, and debate, and profoundly influencing Buddhist logic (hetuvidya).

v.t: Trajan oversees the construction of Rome's aqueduct network
v.s: To address water shortages and sanitation, Emperor Trajan commissioned the new Aqua Traiana and expanded the Aqua Claudia. Roman engineers used arches to build over 60 kilometers of elevated aqueducts, bringing spring water into Rome's public baths, fountains, and private homes. Built of concrete and stone with a gradient of only 30 cm per kilometer, the aqueducts delivered over one billion liters of water daily, enough for a million people. This greatly improved public health and reduced epidemics, serving as an outstanding example of ancient engineering.

v.t: Kushan merchants monopolize the middle section of the Silk Road trade
v.s: Leveraging its strategic location (controlling Central Asia and the Indus Valley) and strong cavalry, the Kushan Empire gradually monopolized transit trade along the central Silk Road. Kushan merchants purchased Chinese silk, lacquerware, and porcelain, transported them via Central Asia to the Persian Gulf, and sold them to Parthian and Roman traders; they also carried Roman glassware, gold coins, Indian spices, and ivory to the East. Cities like Samarkand and Bukhara became international trade hubs, with annual trade worth tens of millions of sesterces. This monopoly brought huge customs revenues to the Kushan Empire, fueling an unprecedented cultural and artistic flourishing.

v.t: Trajan establishes the province of Arabia
v.s: Taking advantage of the power vacuum after the death of the last king of the Nabataean Kingdom, Rabbel II Soter, Emperor Trajan dispatched troops to occupy its capital, Petra, officially incorporating the kingdom into the Roman Empire as the province of Arabia. This gave Rome control over the crucial trade route from the Red Sea to the Mediterranean, bringing the treasury an annual 200 million sesterces from spice trade tariffs alone. Trajan also built the 'Via Traiana Nova' from Aqaba to Damascus, with waystations and garrisons, accelerating the region's Romanization.

v.t: The Church of Antioch rapidly develops and organizes charities
v.s: In the eastern Roman metropolis of Antioch, the Christian church under Bishop Ignatius grew rapidly, with believers reaching over 5,000. The church not only established a refined liturgy and episcopal hierarchy but also founded orphanages, old people's homes, and hospitals to care for the sick, weak, and orphans, attracting many lower-class converts. Antioch became a major center for Christian missionary expansion, and its organizational model and charitable traditions helped pave the way for the Roman Empire's eventual acceptance of Christianity.

v.t: Indirect trade between the Parthian Empire and Eastern Han via the Silk Road
v.s: Although the Parthian Empire and Eastern Han had no direct diplomatic relations, goods like silk, spices, and jewels flowed on a large scale between the two empires through intermediaries such as the Western Regions states and the Kushan Empire. Parthian merchants exchanged Persian Gulf pearls and Syrian glassware for Chinese silks. According to the Book of the Later Han, Parthian envoys presented rare animals like lions and ostriches to the Han court. This trade route enriched the Parthian treasury, enabling it to maintain a large cavalry army to confront Rome.

v.t: Emperor He of Han dies; a hundred-day-old infant ascends as Emperor Shang
v.s: In winter of the 17th year of Yongyuan (105), 27-year-old Emperor He of Han died in Luoyang's Southern Palace, possibly from chronic mercury poisoning from alchemical elixirs. His only son, Liu Long, just 100 days old, was enthroned as Emperor Shang under the regency of Empress Dowager Deng. She assumed power and appointed her brother Deng Zhi as General of Chariots and Cavalry. With the infant emperor, court power fell completely into the hands of the Deng clan, beginning an era of prolonged eunuch and consort clan domination.

v.t: Roman jurists Celsus and Javolenus are active
v.s: During Trajan's reign, jurists Publius Juventius Celsus (and his teacher Javolenus Priscus) became leading legal figures. Celsus served as consul and senator, issuing legal opinions (responsa) on contracts, inheritance, and torts, which were widely cited by judges. His definition of law as 'the art of goodness and equity' became a classic Roman formulation. Their work pushed Roman law away from strict formalism toward flexible, rational, and equitable principles.

v.t: Cai Lun presents improved papermaking to Emperor He of Han
v.s: Cai Lun, the Prefect of the Palace Workshop, synthesized previous papermaking experience, using cheap materials like tree bark, hemp, rags, and old fishing nets. Through processes of soaking, pounding, screening, and drying, he produced high-quality, low-cost plant fiber paper. He personally presented the new paper to Emperor He, who, after testing it, praised it highly and ordered its nationwide promotion. Cai Lun's improved paper replaced heavy bamboo slips and expensive silk, greatly facilitating cultural dissemination and knowledge popularization, revolutionizing human civilization. This paper came to be known as 'Marquis Cai's paper' (Caihou paper).

v.t: Emperor Shang of Han dies young; Empress Dowager Deng enthrones Emperor An
v.s: Emperor Shang died suddenly after only eight months on the throne, either due to congenital weakness or suspected poisoning by Empress Dowager Deng to prolong her rule. Deng and her brother Deng Zhi, General of Chariots and Cavalry, conspired to invite Liu Hu, Prince of Qinghe, to the throne as Emperor An of Han. Liu Hu was only thirteen years old, and Empress Dowager Deng continued to rule as regent. The Deng clan's power reached its zenith, with Deng Zhi promoted to Grand General and seven Deng family members enfeoffed as marquises. The vicious cycle of short-lived, weak emperors and consort clan domination intensified.

v.t: Eastern Han abolishes the Protectorate of the Western Regions and military colonies
v.s: Due to turmoil in the Western Regions, counterattacks by Xiongnu remnants, and financial difficulties, Empress Dowager Deng's court decided to abandon the Western Regions, recalling the Protector Duan Xi and Deputy Colonel Liang Qin, and abolishing military colonies and garrisons. This decision led to the interruption of Han control over the Western Regions, plunging the region back into chaos, with some states reverting to Northern Xiongnu influence. Ban Chao's 30 years of achievements were nearly wiped out, and the Silk Road's security was severely threatened. It would be twenty years before Ban Yong reopened the route.

v.t: Trajan launches the Parthian War
v.s: Using Parthian interference in the Armenian succession as a pretext, Emperor Trajan led ten legions (about 50,000 elite troops) and numerous auxiliaries in a full-scale war against Parthia. The Roman forces advanced on two fronts: the main army from Antioch along the Euphrates to take Armenia; another from Syria into northern Mesopotamia. This eastern campaign was one of the largest military operations of the Roman Empire, as Trajan sought to conquer all of Mesopotamia and emulate the glory of Alexander the Great.

v.t: Trajan promotes urbanization in newly conquered territories
v.s: In newly conquered provinces like Dacia, Arabia, and Armenia, Trajan vigorously promoted Roman-style urban development. He ordered the founding of Roman cities such as Casafana (today's Alba Iulia, Romania) and Philippopolis (today's Plovdiv, Bulgaria), constructing forums, temples, baths, theaters, and arenas, paving roads, and attracting Italian immigrants and retired soldiers to settle. Urbanization accelerated the spread of Roman law, Latin, and Roman lifestyles, fostering a sense of Roman identity in the new provinces.

v.t: Large-scale Qiang rebellion breaks out in Eastern Han
v.s: To campaign in the Western Regions, Empress Dowager Deng's court conscripted Qiang people from commanderies like Longxi, Tianshui, and Jincheng for military service. The Qiang, resentful of oppression and endless campaigns, under leaders Dian Ling and Zhong Qiang, rose in revolt in Longxi, quickly gaining support from Qiang tribes across the region. The rebels captured commanderies such as Longxi, Anding, and Beidi, killed Han officials, and cut off routes to the Western Regions. The Han court was forced to abandon the Western Regions and concentrate on suppression, but suffered repeated defeats. The revolt lasted over a decade, cost the Han 24 billion coins, and marked the decline of the Eastern Han's power.

v.t: Deng Zhi appointed Grand General as regent
v.s: To consolidate consort clan power, Empress Dowager Deng promoted her brother Deng Zhi to Grand General, ranking above the Three Excellences, commanding all military forces. Though from a consort clan, Deng Zhi was modest, humble, and a Confucian scholar. He recommended talents like Yang Zhen and Chen Chan, and advocated tax reduction and disaster relief. However, the Grand General's office wielded immense power; all reports had to be reviewed there before reaching the emperor, worsening the political crisis of consort clan domination.

v.t: Trajan captures Babylon
v.s: During the Parthian War, Emperor Trajan, advancing unstoppably, captured the ancient city of Babylon. This once-great capital of the Neo-Babylonian Empire had long declined, but held strategic military importance. Trajan held a military parade among the ruins of Babylon, proclaiming Rome's irresistible eastward march. Roman forces then continued east, capturing the Parthian capital Ctesiphon and reaching the Persian Gulf. Trajan was the first and only Roman emperor to reach the Persian Gulf.

v.t: Tacitus writes his Histories
v.s: The greatest Roman historian, Publius Cornelius Tacitus, began writing his magnum opus, the Histories. The work, originally twelve books (only the first five and fragments survive), details the history of the Roman Empire from AD 69 to 96, including the Year of the Four Emperors, the founding of the Flavian dynasty by Vespasian, and the tyranny of Domitian. With incisive prose and profound political insight, Tacitus exposed the distortion of human nature and moral decay under autocracy. His other major work, the Annals, covers the period from Tiberius to Nero, together forming the most invaluable historical source for the early Roman Empire.

v.t: Qiang rebels attack the Three Auxiliaries of Guanzhong
v.s: Under leader Dian Ling, the Qiang rebels broke through the Longshan防线 and attacked the Three Auxiliaries (the capital region of Jingzhao, Youfufeng, Zuopingyi), approaching the former Han capital Chang'an. They burned imperial mausoleums and plundered government granaries, alarming the Han court. Empress Dowager Deng urgently dispatched Deng Zun, grandson of General Zhengxi Deng Yu, with 50,000 troops to defend, while ordering local recruitment of soldiers. However, Han troops were demoralized and poorly supplied, suffering repeated defeats. Guanzhong's population was devastated, with people fleeing to Hanzhong and Bashu.

v.t: Eastern Han mobilizes heavy forces to suppress Qiang rebellion
v.s: Facing the intensifying Qiang uprising, Empress Dowager Deng's court appointed General Zhengxi Deng Zun as commander, mobilizing 80,000 central troops, border soldiers, and Southern Xiongnu cavalry, divided into five routes to encircle the rebels. Fierce battles occurred in Longxi, Anding, and elsewhere. Deng Zun combined offers of amnesty with brutal suppression, offering a reward of 10 million coins for the head of rebel leader Dian Ling. Internal divisions emerged among the Qiang, as some tribal leaders killed their own kin for bounty, weakening the rebel cause. However, the ongoing war drained the treasury, forcing the court to impose extra taxes ('mouth tax' and 'land tax'), increasing burdens on the common people.

v.t: Trajan captures the Parthian capital Ctesiphon
v.s: After taking Babylon, Emperor Trajan continued along the Tigris River eastward, arriving at the Parthian capital Ctesiphon. King Osroes I of Parthia fled, and Roman soldiers sacked the palace and treasury, seizing vast amounts of gold and silver accumulated over generations, including the golden lion throne symbolizing Parthian royalty. Trajan held a triumphal entry into Ctesiphon, declaring the incorporation of Parthian territories into Rome as the province of Mesopotamia. This battle marked the greatest extent of the Roman Empire, reaching the Persian Gulf in the east.

v.t: Pliny the Younger publishes his Letters
v.s: Roman author and senator Pliny the Younger compiled his personal correspondence with friends, emperors, and scholars over many years into nine volumes. The letters cover a wide range of topics: daily life, literary criticism, courtroom advocacy, social customs, and political events. Most famous are his two letters to the historian Tacitus, giving a detailed account of the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in AD 79 and the death of his uncle, Pliny the Elder. These firsthand accounts vividly portray Roman society and are invaluable historical documents.

v.t: Border conflict erupts between Goguryeo and Eastern Han
v.s: Taking advantage of the Han's preoccupation with the Qiang rebellion, King Goguryeo (Taejo) led an invasion of the Han commanderies of Liaodong and Xuantu, plundering people and livestock. Liaodong governor Cai Fan led his forces to meet them but was defeated and killed, with thousands of Han soldiers lost. Emperor An was enraged and ordered a counterattack by troops from Xuantu and the Liaodong Dependent State. They defeated the Goguryeo army, recovering lost territory and killing several Goguryeo generals. Goguryeo was forced to sue for peace, returning the kidnapped Han civilians and offering tribute of sable furs and fine horses as tokens of submission. For decades afterward, Goguryeo outwardly complied while secretly expanding its power.

v.t: Eastern Han court debates abandoning Liangzhou
v.s: Due to the ongoing Qiang rebellion and huge costs, some ministers suggested completely abandoning Liangzhou, relocating its people to the interior to shorten defensive lines. This proposal was fiercely opposed by the Gentleman-in-Attendance Yu Xu, who argued that Liangzhou was the shield of the Han; losing it would endanger Guanzhong, and millions of people would be reluctant to leave, causing even greater unrest. Ultimately, Empress Dowager Deng adopted Yu Xu's advice, decided to hold Liangzhou, and appointed Yu Xu as Governor of Wudu to consolidate its defenses. The debate reflected strategic divisions among scholar-officials as Han power declined.

v.t: Anti-Roman uprisings break out across Parthia
v.s: After Trajan's occupation of Mesopotamia, anti-Roman resistance erupted throughout the Parthian Empire. Parthian royals and nobles organized guerrilla warfare in Babylonia, Susa, and elsewhere, attacking Roman supply lines and isolated outposts while the main Roman forces were deployed north. Trajan was forced to march back from the Persian Gulf to suppress them, but could not eliminate all resistance. The tenacious Parthian resistance ultimately forced Hadrian, Trajan's successor, to abandon Mesopotamia.

v.t: Yu Xu consolidates Liangzhou's defense system
v.s: Appointed Governor of Wudu, Yu Xu immediately went to strengthen Liangzhou's defenses. He used a 'multiplying cooking stoves' ruse: ordering soldiers to build twice as many stoves each day, making the Qiang believe reinforcements had arrived. After taking office, he fortified cities, trained troops, recruited refugees for military colonies, and within three years increased Wudu's forces from under 3,000 to 10,000, with ample provisions and repeated victories over the Qiang, stabilizing Liangzhou. Yu Xu also invented the 'horse-powered bellows' and 'water-powered bellows' for smelting and casting weapons, improving military equipment.

v.t: Kushan Buddhist art spreads to the Chinese Western Regions
v.s: Under Kanishka I, Kushan Buddhist art reached its zenith. Numerous Buddha and Bodhisattva statues and sutras were transmitted via the southern Silk Road to Western Regions kingdoms such as Khotan, Kashgar, and Kucha. Artisans there imitated Gandharan styles, carving the first cave temples (e.g., Kizil Caves) and painting Buddhist murals with Hellenistic features. This Buddhist art later provided models for the Mogao Caves and Yungang Grottoes, marking a significant starting point for the Sinification of Buddhism.

v.t: Eastern Han relocates people from frontier commanderies of Liangzhou to the interior
v.s: Under pressure from the ongoing Qiang rebellion, the Han court ordered the forced relocation of people from border commanderies such as Longxi, Anding, Beidi, and Shangjun to the interior (Guanzhong, Hedong, etc.). Troops were sent to drive away reluctant residents, burning their houses and crops. Hundreds of thousands became homeless, and many died of cold, hunger, or Qiang attacks during the migration. This 'abandon the border, protect the interior' policy reduced short-term border pressure, but led to the loss of vast territories, the collapse of border economies, and severe damage to the Han court's prestige.

v.t: Trajan installs a puppet king in Parthia
v.s: To stabilize the occupied Parthian territories, Emperor Trajan installed the pro-Roman Parthian prince Parthamaspates as a puppet king to rule Ctesiphon and its environs. However, this failed to pacify Parthian resistance; most nobles and commoners refused to recognize him and continued resisting. Parthamaspates was killed by rebels after only a few months, dealing a severe setback to Trajan's eastern policy, and Roman forces were forced to withdraw east of the Tigris.

v.t: Qiang rebel forces attack the Three Auxiliaries of Guanzhong
v.s: Qiang rebel leader Dian Ling led tens of thousands of cavalry into the left and right auxiliary regions (Zuopingyi, Youfufeng), reaching the walls of the former Han capital Chang'an. Panic-stricken residents shut themselves up; tombs and palaces outside the city were burned. Emperor An urgently dispatched General Duliang Liang Qin with 20,000 troops to reinforce Chang'an, and after fierce fighting, they repelled the Qiang, temporarily saving the city. However, the Three Auxiliaries were devastated, population plummeted, and vast farmland lay fallow. It was the worst destruction in Guanzhong since the Chimei uprising at the end of the Western Han.

v.t: Qiang leader Dian Ling dies; internal divisions emerge
v.s: The great Qiang rebel leader Dian Ling died of illness during the war, and his son Ling Chang succeeded him. However, Dian Ling's subordinates were not completely obedient to Ling Chang, and internal feuds broke out over leadership and spoils. Some tribal leaders were won over by Deng Zun, accepting Han titles and turning against the uprising. The Qiang's fragmentation allowed the Han to defeat them piecemeal, and the years-long great Qiang rebellion gradually collapsed.

v.t: Eastern Han court issues decree to rectify public finances
v.s: Due to years of costly war against the Qiang, the Eastern Han faced a severe fiscal deficit. Empress Dowager Deng's court ordered a nationwide rectification of finances. The decree required every commandery and county to strictly audit household registrations and farmland, crack down on powerful families hiding population and evading taxes; abolish redundant officials, reduce palace expenditures; and forbid local officials from levying miscellaneous taxes under false pretexts. Though the rectification somewhat increased revenue, it sparked resistance from powerful interests and had limited effect.

v.t: Roman army captures Babylon
v.s: Emperor Trajan sent General Lusius Quietus with forces from Mesopotamia south to capture the ancient city of Babylon, an important river port on the Euphrates. Babylon had lost its political and economic importance, reduced to a small town. The Romans garrisoned the city, establishing a military fort to control river traffic. This was the first and only time in Roman history that they occupied Babylon.

v.t: Inauguration of Trajan's Column
v.s: Emperor Trajan held a grand inauguration ceremony for Trajan's Column in his forum, commemorating his victory in the Dacian Wars. The column stands about 38 meters tall, built from 20 massive blocks of Carrara marble, with an internal spiral staircase of 185 steps leading to the top. The column's shaft is wrapped in a 200-meter-long spiral frieze depicting scenes from the wars: crossing the Danube, besieging enemy cities, executing captives – 155 scenes with about 2,600 figures. A statue of Trajan originally stood atop (replaced with St. Peter in the 16th century). The column is a masterpiece of Roman art and engineering and a model for later commemorative monuments.

v.t: Emperor An of Han changes era name to Yuanchu
v.s: Praying for peace and stability and to end the Qiang rebellion, Emperor An decreed a change of era name to 'Yuanchu' (Primary Beginning). The change included a general amnesty (except for heinous crimes), a reduction of one degree in punishments for prisoners, and an order for local authorities to recommend 'filial and incorrupt' and 'flourishing talent' candidates for office, while also forgiving back taxes owed by the poor. However, the era change did little to change the Eastern Han's internal and external difficulties: the Qiang revolt continued, and the treasury remained empty.

v.t: Rome makes Armenia a province
v.s: Emperor Trajan abolished the Parthian-appointed king of Armenia and formally annexed it as a Roman province, ruled directly by a Roman governor. This completely integrated Armenia into Rome's sphere of influence, strengthening its strategic position in the Caucasus and providing a rear base for future competition with Parthia over Mesopotamia. However, the annexation triggered a strong backlash from the Parthian Empire, and Armenian nobles also repeatedly revolted against Roman rule.

v.t: Eastern Han army defeats Qiang forces in Hexi Corridor
v.s: General Duliang Liang Qin and Wudu Governor Yu Xu combined forces to engage the main Qiang army near Zhangye and Jiuquan in the Hexi Corridor. Using a combination of cavalry charges and infantry phalanxes, the Han achieved a major victory, killing over 3,000 Qiang, capturing more than 10,000, and seizing over 100,000 horses, cattle, and sheep. Qiang leader Ling Chang fled with his remaining forces deep into the desert. This was the greatest Han victory in the Qiang war to date, and the situation in Liangzhou began to improve.

v.t: Hadrian succeeds as Roman emperor
v.s: Emperor Trajan died of illness during his Parthian campaign at Selinus in Asia Minor. On his deathbed, he adopted his cousin Hadrian as his son and designated him successor. Hadrian, then serving as governor of Syria in Antioch, quickly returned to Rome, where the Senate, under pressure from the army, recognized him as emperor. Hadrian immediately reversed Trajan's expansionist policy, abandoning hard-to-defend eastern territories like Mesopotamia and adopting a defensive strategy, building walls to strengthen the borders.

v.t: Eastern Han defeats the remaining Xianling Qiang
v.s: General Zhengxi Deng Zun, commanding a combined force of Han troops and Southern Xiongnu cavalry, crushed the remaining Xianling Qiang forces at Lingwu Valley (present-day Lingwu, Ningxia). Qiang leader Ling Chang was assassinated by one of his own officers, and his head was sent to Luoyang. Previously rebellious Qiang tribes now surrendered one after another, and the Han regained control over most of Liangzhou. However, over a decade of war had reduced Liangzhou's population by 60%, collapsed its economy, and severely weakened its border defenses.

v.t: Roman Emperor Hadrian abandons Mesopotamia
v.s: New Emperor Hadrian considered Trajan's conquests in Mesopotamia difficult and costly to defend, especially given ongoing Parthian uprisings. He therefore ordered a Roman withdrawal from Mesopotamia back to the west bank of the Euphrates. Hadrian made peace with the Parthian king, recognizing Parthian sovereignty over Mesopotamia, while Armenia became a buffer state between Rome and Parthia. Though criticized by pro-war senators, the move effectively shortened Rome's defensive lines, allowing concentration of forces against barbarian threats in Europe.

v.t: Jewish uprising in Babylonia
v.s: Influenced by Trajan's eastern campaign and the upheaval in the Roman Empire, Jews in Babylonia launched a large-scale revolt against Roman rule. They attacked Roman garrisons, occupied cities, and coordinated with other Jewish rebels. Roman general Lusius Quietus brutally suppressed the revolt, destroying Jewish communities in Babylon and elsewhere, killing or enslaving tens of thousands. This uprising further worsened Jewish-Roman relations and set the stage for the later Bar Kokhba revolt.

v.t: Hadrian inspects the province of Britain
v.s: Emperor Hadrian toured the province of Britain to assess the defenses of the northern border. He found that the Picts in northern Britain were raiding south frequently, while Roman defenses were weak. Hadrian decided to build a wall across the northern part of Britain to block barbarian incursions. He established the provincial capital at York and personally selected the route for the wall. This structure became known as Hadrian's Wall, one of the most famous frontier defenses of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Xianbei chief Tanshihuai unifies the steppe tribes
v.s: Xianbei chief Tanshihuai convened a tribal assembly at Mount Danhan (present-day Shangdu, Inner Mongolia). Through conquest and marriage alliances, he unified the Xianbei, Wuhuan, and remnants of the Northern Xiongnu into a powerful steppe empire. He established three divisions (east, central, west) under commanders, commanding over 100,000 cavalry. After unifying the Xianbei, Tanshihuai repeatedly raided the Eastern Han border, becoming the most serious frontier threat in the mid-to-late Eastern Han.

v.t: Hadrian formally abandons Trajan's eastern territories
v.s: Emperor Hadrian concluded a peace treaty with Parthia, formally abandoning most of the Mesopotamian and Armenian territories conquered by Trajan, and restoring the eastern borders of the Augustan era. Hadrian also disbanded three new legions raised by Trajan and reduced garrisons. This marked a shift in Roman strategy from expansion to consolidation; thereafter, Rome's borders remained largely stable for a century.

v.t: Construction of Hadrian's Wall officially begins
v.s: Emperor Hadrian personally presided over the foundation ceremony of Hadrian's Wall. The wall stretched from the River Tyne in the east to the Solway Firth in the west, about 117 kilometers long. It was built of stone and turf, about 4.5 meters high and 2.5-3 meters wide, with a milecastle every Roman mile and two turrets between milecastles. A ditch about 3 meters deep was dug north of the wall. The project employed about 15,000 men from three legions and took six years to complete. Hadrian's Wall became the strongest northern barrier of the Roman Empire, effectively checking Pictish incursions.

v.t: Zhang Heng appointed Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau (Taishiling)
v.s: The eminent scientist and writer Zhang Heng was appointed by Emperor An as Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau (Taishiling), responsible for astronomical observations, calendar revision, and historiography. Zhang immediately set about improving the armillary sphere and recalculating celestial positions. During his tenure, he also began work on an earthquake detector (seismoscope) and wrote astronomical works such as the Lingxian. The position provided Zhang with a platform for systematic scientific experimentation and observation.

v.t: Hadrian inspects the Gaul and German defenses
v.s: After inspecting Britain, Emperor Hadrian toured the Roman defenses in the provinces of Gaul and Germany. He ordered the construction of a series of forts, fortresses, and watchtowers along the Rhine and Danube rivers, connected by wooden palisades to form a unified border defense system (the Upper Germanic-Rhaetian Limes). Hadrian also strengthened the training and discipline of local garrisons, dismissed corrupt officers, and improved the combat effectiveness of the border troops.

v.t: Empress Dowager Deng promotes official schools throughout the country
v.s: Empress Dowager Deng (Hexi) issued a decree requiring each commandery, kingdom, and county to establish official schools to teach the Confucian classics. She believed that 'the foundation of education lies in learning.' She personally wrote textbooks such as Admonitions for Women and had copies of classics in the palace library distributed to the commanderies. The court also decreed that students passing exams could be exempted from corvée labor, and outstanding ones could be recommended as 'filial and incorrupt.' This policy promoted the spread of education in the mid-Eastern Han, cultivating many Confucian scholars, but due to local financial difficulties, implementation was limited.

v.t: Hadrian tours Greece, promoting a cultural revival
v.s: Emperor Hadrian made massive cultural investments in Athens. He completed the Temple of Olympian Zeus (after 600+ years of construction), built a new library, a gymnasium, and an aqueduct, and improved urban infrastructure. Hadrian, an ardent admirer of Greek culture, wore Greek robes, participated in the Eleusinian Mysteries, and established the Panhellenion in Athens to promote cultural exchange among Greek city-states. Hadrian's promotion of Greek culture sparked a 'Greek cultural renaissance' (Second Sophistic) in the Roman Empire, with Greek rhetoric, philosophy, and literature becoming fashionable again among the empire's educated elite.

v.t: Emperor An of Han changes era name to Yongning
v.s: Praying for peace and stability on the frontiers and the empire's longevity, Emperor An decreed a change of era name to 'Yongning' (Eternal Peace). A general amnesty was declared, along with a one-grade elevation of commoners' rank and partial tax reductions. However, peace did not come: Xianbei and Wuhuan raided the borders year after year; though the Qiang uprising had subsided, Liangzhou remained devastated. The Eastern Han dynasty still struggled with internal and external troubles.

v.t: Envoys from the Shan Kingdom (present-day Myanmar) pay tribute to Han
v.s: King Yong Youdiao of the Shan Kingdom (in today's Shan State, Myanmar) sent envoys via Yongchang Commandery to Luoyang, presenting rare animals such as elephants, rhinos, peacocks, as well as ivory and jadeite to Emperor An. This is a rare record of direct contact between the Eastern Han and a Southeast Asian state, reflecting the openness of the southern Silk Road. Emperor An enfeoffed the Shan envoy as 'Han Grand Commandant', granting a gold seal, purple cord, and silk, and allowed him to stay in Luoyang to study Han culture.

v.t: Eunuch Li Run and wet nurse Wang Sheng control the government
v.s: After Empress Dowager Deng's death, Emperor An of Han took personal control. He resented Deng's long regency and favored the eunuch Li Run and his wet nurse Wang Sheng, who had looked after him as a child. Wang Sheng and her daughter Bo Rong, relying on the emperor's favor, sold offices and interfered in justice. Eunuch Li Run controlled the imperial guards and the drafting of edicts. The Deng consort clan was purged: Deng Zhi and six others were dismissed, and many Deng relatives executed. Power shifted from consort clan to eunuchs, and Eastern Han politics became even more corrupt.

v.t: Grand Commandant Yang Zhen commits suicide after remonstrating
v.s: Grand Commandant Yang Zhen repeatedly memorialized Emperor An, impeaching eunuch Li Run, wet nurse Wang Sheng, and others for corruption and meddling in state affairs, urging the emperor to favor worthy ministers. Not only did the emperor ignore him, but he also accused Yang of slandering the court, dismissed him, and ordered him to return home. On the way, at the Xi Yang Pavilion west of Luoyang, Yang said to his disciples: 'Death is the common fate of a scholar. I was fortunate to hold the highest office, yet I could not eliminate traitorous ministers. How can I face the sun and moon again?' He then took poison and died. Yang's death aroused public anger among scholar-officials, and later generations called him 'Confucius of Guanxi'.

v.t: Empress Dowager Deng dies; Emperor An of Han begins personal rule
v.s: Empress Dowager Deng (Hexi) died of illness in Luoyang at age 41. Although she had tried hard to maintain Eastern Han rule, she failed to solve the Qiang rebellion, fiscal problems, and the cycle of eunuch-consort domination. After her death, Emperor An formally took personal power and immediately purged the Deng consort clan, dismissing Deng Zhi and executing many Deng relatives. The Eastern Han government shifted from consort clan rule to alternating consort and eunuch tyranny.

v.t: Hadrian tours Egypt
v.s: Emperor Hadrian toured the province of Egypt, visiting the cities of Alexandria, Memphis, and Thebes. At the Library of Alexandria, he listened to scholars' reports and funded research projects. During the tour, his male lover Antinous drowned in the Nile. Hadrian was devastated, ordered Antinous deified, and built temples for him throughout Egypt. This event promoted the fusion of Greco-Roman and Egyptian religions.

v.t: Emperor An of Han changes era name to Yanguang
v.s: Hoping to prolong the dynasty's fortunes, Emperor An changed the era name to 'Yanguang' (Extended Light). At the same time, he established Lady Song as empress, enfeoffed his wet nurse Wang Sheng as 'Lady of Yejun', and ennobled eunuch Li Run as a marquis, granting them large estates, mansions, and slaves. Upright officials were increasingly marginalized, and corruption worsened.

v.t: Ban Yong leads forces to re-open the Western Regions
v.s: Ban Yong, son of Ban Chao, was appointed by Emperor An as Chief Clerk of the Western Regions. Leading 500 troops stationed at Liuzhong (today's Shanshan, Xinjiang), Ban Yong allied with states like Shanshan and Kucha to defeat the Northern Xiongnu and Cheshi coalition, reopening the northern Silk Road. He then gradually recovered Karasahr and Kashgar, and over fifty Western Regions states again submitted to the Han. Ban Yong recorded his experiences, providing the foundational material for the Book of the Later Han's account of the Western Regions.

v.t: Rome strengthens its German defensive line
v.s: Emperor Hadrian ordered the reinforcement of the German-Rhaetian Limes (the Germanic line) between the Rhine and Danube. The project included over 600 new watchtowers, over 100 small forts, plus palisades and ditches. The total length was about 550 kilometers, with garrisons and supply stations along the way. This line effectively monitored Germanic tribe movements and maintained relative peace on the border for over forty years.

v.t: Xianbei raid Eastern Han borders year after year
v.s: After unifying, the Xianbei under Tanshihuai launched large-scale invasions of the Han's Youzhou, Bingzhou, and Liangzhou every autumn. They plundered people, livestock, and grain, burned towns, causing great suffering. Emperor An mobilized tens of thousands of border troops, but the Xianbei cavalry were swift and elusive; the Han forces were repeatedly defeated, suffering heavy losses. In the second year of Yanguang alone, the Xianbei carried off over 2,000 Han households and tens of thousands of livestock.

v.t: Emperor An of Han deposes Crown Prince Liu Bao
v.s: Believing slanders from eunuch Li Run and wet nurse Wang Sheng that Crown Prince Liu Bao (son of Consort Li) was plotting rebellion, Emperor An, at only ten years old, was deposed as Prince of Jiyin. This violated the patriarchal rule of 'establishing the eldest son of the empress,' arousing discontent at court. Lai Li and over ten others petitioned for reversal, but not only did the emperor ignore them, he dismissed them from office. After the prince's deposition, Empress Yan became even more dictatorial, and the political situation worsened.

v.t: Ban Yong defeats the Xiongnu coalition in the Western Regions
v.s: Ban Yong, leading a coalition of Han troops and forces from Shanshan, Kucha, and other Western Regions states, decisively defeated a Northern Xiongnu and Cheshi Later Kingdom coalition at the Jiaohe city (present-day Turpan, Xinjiang), killing over 2,000 and capturing the king of Cheshi Later Kingdom. Ban Yong then pursued, recovering Cheshi Earlier Kingdom and Gaochang, re-establishing control over the north-south routes through the Tian Shan. This victory consolidated the second Han operation in the Western Regions, and Ban Yong was enfeoffed as Marquis Dingyuan for his achievements.

v.t: Eunuch Sun Cheng et al. stage coup to enthrone Emperor Shun of Han
v.s: After Emperor An died during a southern tour, Empress Dowager Yan and Yan Xian enthroned Liu Yi, Marquis of Beixiang, who died soon after. Nineteen eunuchs led by Sun Cheng and Wang Kang conspired and staged a palace coup, killing Yan Xian and his brothers, imprisoning Empress Dowager Yan, and welcoming the deposed prince Liu Bao as Emperor Shun of Han. All nineteen eunuchs were enfeoffed as marquises for their role, known as the 'Nineteen Marquises'. This event opened the door to large-scale enfeoffment of eunuchs and their interference in imperial succession. Eunuch power henceforth became a major political force in the Eastern Han.

v.t: Emperor An of Han dies during southern inspection tour
v.s: Emperor An, accompanied by Empress Yan, toured Jingzhou and Wancheng. On the return to Luoyang, he suddenly died at Yexian (today's Ye County, Henan) at age 32. Cause of death may have been sudden illness, or some historians suspect poisoning by Empress Yan and eunuchs. After his death, Empress Yan kept the news secret, hurriedly recalled her brother Yan Xian to the capital, enthroned Liu Yi, Marquis of Beixiang, and herself served as regent.

v.t: Reconstruction of the Pantheon in Rome completed
v.s: Emperor Hadrian rebuilt the Pantheon on the site of Agrippa's original temple from the Augustan era. The new Pantheon features the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, 43.3 meters in diameter, equal to its height, forming a perfect spherical space. At the apex is a 9-meter-wide oculus, the only source of natural light, symbolizing the connection between gods and mortals. The Pantheon is the highest achievement of Roman architectural technology and art; it remains well preserved and is used as a church.

v.t: Hadrian builds his mausoleum in Rome
v.s: Emperor Hadrian began construction of his own mausoleum on the west bank of the Tiber (the Castel Sant'Angelo). The base is a square approximately 90 meters on each side, topped by a cylinder about 70 meters in diameter. Atop stood a gilded bronze statue of Hadrian in a quadriga (chariot). The interior features complex annular and spiral corridors, and the exterior was decorated with marble and statues. The mausoleum was later converted into a fortress by the popes and connected to the Vatican by a corridor.

v.t: Emperor Shun of Han purges the Yan consort clan
v.s: After being enthroned with the support of eunuchs like Sun Cheng, Emperor Shun purged the Yan consort clan. Empress Dowager Yan was moved to a detached palace and placed under house arrest; Yan Xian and his brothers were executed; Yan family members were exiled. However, the victorious eunuchs soon became tyrannical themselves. Although Emperor Shun wanted to free himself from eunuch control, he was indebted to them for his succession and could only secretly cultivate other eunuchs to counterbalance them. The cycle of consort and eunuch tyranny continued.

v.t: Ban Yong defeats the Northern Xiongnu
v.s: Ban Yong led a combined Han and Western Regions force of over 20,000 to Jincheng (present-day Jimsar, Xinjiang) north of the Tian Shan, where they crushed the Northern Xiongnu's Huyan prince, killing over a thousand and capturing tens of thousands of livestock. The remaining Northern Xiongnu fled west of the Altai Mountains and did not dare to return east for over a decade. Ban Yong then recovered Cheshi Later Kingdom and reestablished the Garrison Commandant (Wuji Xiaowei), stabilizing the Western Regions.

v.t: Hadrian returns to Rome after long inspection tour
v.s: Emperor Hadrian ended his years-long imperial inspection tour and returned to Rome. He had visited most of the provinces: Britain, Gaul, Germany, Spain, Greece, Asia Minor, Egypt, and others, meeting local officials, inspecting troops, and funding public works. This system of tours strengthened central control over provinces and provided firsthand knowledge, a hallmark of Hadrian's rule. After returning, he focused on urban construction, building the Temple of Venus and Roma and other structures.

v.t: Ban Yong negotiates submission of Western Regions states
v.s: Using military victories and political persuasion, Ban Yong successfully induced the remaining states of Karasahr, Weili, and Weixu to submit. These states sent princes to Luoyang as hostages and accepted Han investiture. Ban Yong also reestablished the Protectorate of the Western Regions and the military colony system, restoring Han administrative control. All fifty-plus Western Regions states now again submitted to the Eastern Han, and the Silk Road was fully open.

v.t: Hadrian issues the Perpetual Edict
v.s: Emperor Hadrian commissioned the eminent jurist Salvius Julianus to systematically compile and codify all past praetorian edicts into a comprehensive work, the Perpetual Edict (Edictum Perpetuum). It synthesized core Roman law on procedure, property, contracts, inheritance, torts, and more, and stipulated that future praetors must follow the Edict and not create new rules. This codification was a major milestone in Roman law, moving from case-by-case interpretation to systematic codification, deeply influencing later civil law systems.

v.t: Hadrian completes the Pantheon's dome
v.s: The Pantheon, rebuilt by Emperor Hadrian, was officially completed, its dome the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the ancient world. Engineers used a technique of progressively lighter aggregates: at the base, heavy tufa and basalt; at the top, lightweight pumice and even empty amphorae. The dome's thickness tapers from 6 meters at the base to 1.5 meters at the oculus. The oculus creates a shifting beam of light, producing a sacred, ever-changing atmosphere. The Pantheon is still in use today, a pinnacle of Roman architectural technology.

v.t: Frequent earthquakes in Luoyang area
v.s: The Eastern Han capital Luoyang and its surrounding areas experienced multiple strong earthquakes, collapsing countless houses and causing heavy casualties. According to the Book of the Later Han, during the earthquakes, 'the capital shook, the ground split open, and water gushed out.' Sections of the city wall and palace buildings collapsed. Emperor Shun issued relief decrees, distributing grain and medicine, and pardoned prisoners to avert calamity. However, the frequent earthquakes were seen by the court as a warning from heaven, intensifying political crisis; eunuchs and consort relatives accused each other of being the root cause.

v.t: Eastern Han restores administrative structure in Liangzhou
v.s: As the Qiang rebellion subsided, the Han court ordered the restoration of Liangzhou's administrative structure, reestablishing the provincial inspector (zhou mu) and commandery governors, and encouraging the return of displaced people. The court provided seeds, oxen, and tools to returning families and exempted them from taxes for three years. The border defense system was also rebuilt, recruiting Qiang locals as 'righteous troops' (yicong jun) to help garrison the frontier. Liangzhou's society and economy slowly recovered, but its strategic importance was not as great as during the Western Han.

v.t: Kushan Empire and Eastern Han exchange envoys
v.s: Emperor Kanishka I of the Kushan Empire dispatched a delegation bearing Buddhist scriptures and treasures to Luoyang, seeking an audience with Emperor Shun. They requested that the Han send eminent monks to Kushan to spread the Dharma, and presented Buddha relics and statues to the Han court. Emperor Shun reciprocated with silk, lacquerware, and bronze mirrors, and sent envoys to Kushan. This diplomatic contact facilitated the further eastward transmission of Buddhism and Gandharan art along the Silk Road, setting the stage for Buddhism's large-scale introduction to China.

v.t: Hadrian tours the North African provinces
v.s: Emperor Hadrian toured the North African provinces of Numidia and Africa, inspecting irrigation systems and urban defenses. He commissioned the construction of the 'Via Hadriana' from Carthage to Tacapae (Gabès), improving transport links between North Africa and Italy. Hadrian also encouraged the spread of Roman agricultural techniques in North Africa, expanding olive and grain cultivation, making the region an important granary for the Roman Empire.

v.t: Zhang Heng drafts the seismoscope design
v.s: After years of studying earthquakes and mechanical design, Zhang Heng, Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau, produced detailed drawings of a 'wind-weather seismoscope' (houfeng didong yi). The device was to be cast in bronze, shaped like a wine jar. Inside, a central 'pillar' (inverted pendulum) was connected to eight sets of levers and cranks, each linked to a bronze dragon holding a bronze ball. When an earthquake occurred, the pillar would fall in the direction of the tremor, triggering the lever to open the dragon's mouth, dropping the ball into a waiting toad's mouth, thus indicating the earthquake's direction. After the drawings were completed, Zhang began casting the instrument.

v.t: Eastern Han expands the Imperial University on a large scale
v.s: Emperor Shun, acting on the advice of Minister Zuo Xiong, allocated funds to expand the Imperial University (Taixue) in Luoyang. The project took two years, adding 240 building complexes and 1,850 classrooms and dormitories, capable of accommodating 30,000 students. The university taught the Five Classics of Confucianism (Poetry, Documents, Rites, Changes, Spring and Autumn) under the instruction of academicians (boshi) and classicists. After expansion, the Taixue became the largest institution of higher learning in the world at the time, attracting scholars from all over, creating the spectacle of 'thirty thousand Taixue students.' These students later played important roles in the 'Pure Criticism' (Qingyi) movement and the 'Disasters of Partisan Prohibitions' (Danggu).

v.t: Zhang Heng invents and constructs an armillary sphere driven by water
v.s: At Luoyang, Zhang Heng completed a water-powered armillary sphere (hun tian yi). Made of bronze with a diameter of about 4.6 chi, the sphere was engraved with constellations, the celestial equator, the ecliptic, and other astronomical markings. Driven by a waterwheel and gear system, the sphere could simulate the daily motion of the celestial sphere, showing the rising and setting of stars and their paths, largely in sync with actual sky observations. This was the world's first water-powered astronomical demonstration instrument, representing the highest level of ancient mechanical engineering and astronomy.

v.t: Zhang Heng invents and tests the seismoscope
v.s: Zhang Heng, Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau, completed the world's first seismoscope – the 'houfeng didong yi' – in Luoyang. The device was cast in bronze, eight chi in diameter, shaped like a wine jar, with intricate internal mechanisms. In 133 (or slightly later), the seismoscope successfully recorded an earthquake in Longxi (not felt by people in Luoyang), verifying its effectiveness. The seismoscope predated the Western seismoscope (18th century) by about 1600 years, a remarkable achievement in the history of ancient technology.

v.t: Emperor Shun reforms the recommendation system (Chaju)
v.s: Acting on the advice of Minister Zuo Xiong, Emperor Shun introduced major reforms to the Chaju (recommendation) system. It decreed that candidates for 'filial and incorrupt' must be at least forty years old and must pass an examination in the 'Five Classics' and administrative writing, administered by the court, before being appointed to office. This reform aimed to break the monopoly of powerful families over recommendations and raise official quality. However, it aroused strong resistance from local elites, and the policy was soon abandoned in practice.

v.t: The Bar Kokhba Jewish revolt breaks out
v.s: Emperor Hadrian founded a Roman colony called 'Aelia Capitolina' on the ruins of Jerusalem and built a temple to Jupiter on the Temple Mount, while also forbidding Jews from practicing circumcision and observing the Sabbath. These policies outraged the Jews, and under the leadership of Simon Bar Kokhba (meaning 'Son of the Star'), they launched their last major revolt against Roman rule. The rebels quickly seized most of Judaea, established an independent state, and minted their own coins. Bar Kokhba was hailed as the Messiah by the Jews, and the revolt grew dramatically in scale.

v.t: Hadrian mobilizes heavy forces to suppress the Jewish revolt
v.s: Emperor Hadrian redeployed 12 legions (about 120,000 troops) from Britain, Germany, and other frontiers, under General Julius Severus, to Judaea to crush the Bar Kokhba revolt. The Romans used scorched-earth tactics, gradually tightening the siege on Jewish strongholds. Bar Kokhba's forces resisted fiercely, fighting guerrilla warfare from caves and mountains, inflicting heavy casualties on the Roman army. The war lasted three years, becoming one of the most brutal suppressions in Roman history.

v.t: Seismoscope successfully records the Longxi earthquake
v.s: Zhang Heng's seismoscope successfully detected an earthquake in Longxi. At the time, no one in Luoyang felt any tremor, but the seismoscope dropped a ball from the dragon facing west. Days later, a courier reported the earthquake from Longxi, confirming the instrument's accuracy. The court and public were amazed, and Emperor Shun issued an edict praising Zhang Heng. The seismoscope was the world's first instrument for measuring the direction of earthquakes, predating similar Western inventions by some 1,700 years.

v.t: Roman army captures the last Jewish stronghold
v.s: After three years of brutal war, the Roman army captured the last Jewish fortress of Betar. The Romans massacred the defenders and civilians; Bar Kokhba died in battle. It is estimated that the Romans destroyed 50 forts and 985 villages, killing about 580,000 Jews, with many more enslaved. Hadrian erased the name Judaea, renaming it 'Syria Palaestina', and forbade Jews from entering Jerusalem. The Jewish people began the 'Great Diaspora', lasting over 1,800 years.

v.t: Eastern Han reforms the palace harem system
v.s: To reduce palace expenses, Emperor Shun ordered a reduction in the numbers of palace ladies and eunuchs. The number of palace ladies was cut from over 5,000 to 2,000, and eunuchs from 3,000 to 1,800. He also regulated the ranks and treatment of consorts and forbade eunuchs from interfering in state affairs. However, this did not stop eunuch influence: eunuchs like Sun Cheng still controlled the imperial guard and edict drafting, and were locked in power struggles with the Liang consort clan.

v.t: Liang Shang appointed Grand General as regent
v.s: To counterbalance eunuch power, Emperor Shun established the daughter of Liang Shang as empress and appointed Liang Shang as Grand General, giving him full control of the government. Liang Shang was modest, cautious, and a Confucian scholar, appointing Li Gu, Zhou Ju, and other worthy ministers, gaining respect. However, Liang Shang's son Liang Ji was violent and self-serving, laying the groundwork for future tyranny under the Liang consort clan.

v.t: Zhang Heng petitions to ban apocryphal texts (Chenwei)
v.s: Zhang Heng, Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau, submitted a memorial to Emperor Shun requesting a ban on apocryphal texts (chenwei divination). He argued that such texts were invented by frauds under the name of Confucius, misleading the people and endangering governance. Zhang asked the court to burn all chenwei books and forbid scholars from studying or spreading them. Although Emperor Shun did not accept the proposal, Zhang Heng's critique represented the rationalist Confucian reaction against mysticism in the Eastern Han.

v.t: Zhang Heng retires to his hometown and writes
v.s: Due to age and illness, Zhang Heng resigned his post as Prefect of the Grand Astrology Bureau and returned to his hometown in Xi'e County, Nanyang Commandery (present-day Nanyang, Henan). There he focused on literary and philosophical works, composing the 'Yingjian' (Responses to Critiques), 'SXuan Fu' (Rhapsody on Pondering the Profound), and 'Guitian Fu' (Rhapsody on Returning to the Fields), summarizing his academic achievements and completing the 'Xuan Tu' (Diagram of the Profound). Zhang Heng died in 139. His contributions to astronomy, geography, mechanics, mathematics, and literature make him the most versatile scientist of the Eastern Han.

v.t: Emperor Shun changes era name to Yonghe
v.s: Praying for harmony in the empire, Emperor Shun changed the era name to 'Yonghe' (Eternal Harmony). A general amnesty was declared, and commoners were granted one rank of nobility, with partial tax reductions. However, during the Yonghe years, the Western Qiang rebelled again, and the Southern Xiongnu were restless; the Han borders remained unstable.

v.t: Eastern Han and Southern Xiongnu jointly campaign against Xianbei
v.s: General Duliang Ma Xu and the Southern Xiongnu Left Wise King combined forces in two columns to campaign north against the Xianbei. Han troops left Yunzhong Commandery, and Southern Xiongnu cavalry left Dingxiang Commandery, attacking Tanshihuai's Xianbei from east and west. The two sides clashed at Baidao (north of present-day Hohhot, Inner Mongolia), resulting in a decisive victory for the Han-Xiongnu coalition, with over a thousand Xianbei killed and hundreds of thousands of horses, cattle, and sheep captured. The Xianbei were forced to retreat north of the Gobi, temporarily halting their raids on Han territory.

v.t: Antoninus Pius succeeds as Roman emperor
v.s: Emperor Hadrian, sensing his death approaching, adopted the senator Antoninus Pius as his son and heir. Hadrian also stipulated that Antoninus must adopt both Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus as his own successors, establishing the 'adoptive succession' system. Hadrian died later that year, and Antoninus Pius succeeded without incident. His reign (138–161) was one of the most peaceful and prosperous in Roman history.

v.t: Southern Xiongnu collude with Wuhuan in rebellion
v.s: Some Southern Xiongnu tribes, resentful of the Han's conscription of their cavalry against the Xianbei, allied with the Wuhuan in rebellion. The rebels captured commanderies such as Wuyuan and Shuofang, killed Han officials, and plundered the population. Emperor Shun ordered Ma Xu to suppress the revolt, which took several months of fierce fighting. The Southern Xiongnu Left Wise King was executed, and the rebel tribes were broken up and resettled. This revolt revealed the weakening of Han control over the Southern Xiongnu.

v.t: Eastern Han suffers defeat in Qiang campaign
v.s: The Western Qiang, led by the Zhong Qiang tribe together with the Shaodang Qiang, rebelled again, raiding Longxi and Jincheng. General Zhengxi Ma Xian led a campaign against them but was ambushed at Shegu Mountain (near present-day Lintao, Gansu). Ma Xian and his two sons were killed, and thousands of Han troops were lost. This defeat plunged Liangzhou back into war, alarming the Han court, which was forced to send reinforcements.

v.t: Rome repairs Hadrian's Wall
v.s: Emperor Antoninus Pius ordered a major repair and reinforcement of Hadrian's Wall in Britain. Sections of the wall that had fallen into disrepair were rebuilt in stone, over a dozen watchtowers and forts were added, and new ditches were dug. After the repairs, the Wall's defensive capabilities were greatly enhanced, effectively checking the southward incursions of the Picts.

v.t: Severe famine strikes Eastern Han
v.s: Heavy rains caused the Yellow River to flood in Qingzhou, Xuzhou, and Yanzhou, inundating hundreds of thousands of hectares of cropland. Meanwhile, Bingzhou and Liangzhou suffered severe drought, with no harvest. Millions of refugees faced starvation; corpses littered the roads, and cannibalism occurred. Emperor Shun ordered the opening of granaries for relief and allocated five million coins from the treasury to purchase grain, but it was insufficient. The famine exacerbated social tensions, and peasant uprisings arose everywhere.

v.t: Southern Xiongnu and Wuhuan revolt together
v.s: The Southern Xiongnu leaders Wusi and Cheniu, together with Wuhuan and Xianbei tribes, launched a large-scale revolt, besieging Xihc and Shangjun commanderies and killing the General Duliang Chen Gui. Emperor Shun ordered the Colonel of the Xiongnu (Shi Xiongnu Zhonglang Jiang) Zhang Dan to lead a campaign. Both sides suffered heavy losses over several months, plunging the northern border into prolonged instability.

v.t: Eastern Han suppresses the Southern Xiongnu revolt
v.s: After more than a year of fighting, Colonel Zhang Dan eventually defeated and killed the Southern Xiongnu rebel leader Wusi. He then induced the surrender of other rebel leaders like Cheniu, reasserting control over the Southern Xiongnu. However, the Southern Xiongnu were greatly weakened, making them more dependent on Han protection, while the Han had spent heavily on military campaigns, worsening its fiscal situation.

v.t: Liang Ji succeeds his father Liang Shang as Grand General
v.s: After the death of Grand General Liang Shang, his son Liang Ji was appointed Grand General, taking full control of the government. Liang Ji was cruel, violent, and greedy, the opposite of his father. He immediately began eliminating rivals, installing his own cronies, and selling official positions, turning the court into the private property of the Liang clan. Eastern Han politics now entered the darkest period of Liang Ji's tyranny.

v.t: Zhang Gang impeaches Liang Ji, shaking the court
v.s: The Attendant Censor (Shi Yushi) Zhang Gang was ordered to tour various regions to inspect official conduct. But as soon as he left Luoyang, he destroyed his carriage, saying, 'The wolf is in the road, why ask about the foxes?' He then returned and submitted a memorial impeaching Grand General Liang Ji on fifteen counts, including 'monopolizing power, acting tyrannically, corruption, and harming the loyal.' The memorial sent shockwaves through the court and among the populace. Emperor Shun, however, dared not punish Liang Ji; instead, he demoted Zhang Gang to be Grand Administrator of Guangling. Liang Ji bore a grudge but dared not harm Zhang because of his reputation.

v.t: Emperor Shun changes era name to Han'an
v.s: Hoping to bring peace to the Han, Emperor Shun changed the era name to 'Han'an' (Han Peace). However, the era change did nothing to stop the worsening political corruption and social conflicts; Liang Ji's power only grew stronger.

v.t: Zhang Gang appointed Governor of Guangling, convinces rebel leader to surrender
v.s: After being demoted to Governor of Guangling, Zhang Gang rode alone to the camp of local peasant rebel leader Zhang Ying. He explained the consequences and the benefits of peace. Zhang Ying, impressed by Zhang Gang's courage and sincerity, surrendered with tens of thousands of followers. Zhang Gang resettled the surrendered people, selected capable officials, and brought peace to Guangling. However, Zhang Gang soon died of illness at age 36. Zhang Ying and others mourned him bitterly. Even Liang Ji, though he hated Zhang Gang, had to admit his talent.

v.t: Kushan Empire reaches cultural zenith
v.s: Under Kanishka I and his successors, the Kushan Empire entered its golden age. Its capital, Peshawar (present-day Pakistan), became a Buddhist center, with the Kanishka Stupa, about 200 meters high, one of the tallest structures in the world at that time. Gandharan art, blending Greek, Persian, and Indian styles, produced numerous Buddha statues and narrative reliefs. The Kushans also controlled the central Silk Road trade, enjoyed economic prosperity, and cultural diversity, becoming one of the four great empires of the world at the time (along with Rome, Parthia, and Han China).

v.t: Emperor Shun of Han dies; Emperor Chong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Shun died of illness in Luoyang at age 30. His son Liu Bing succeeded as Emperor Chong, only two years old. Empress Dowager Liang served as regent, and her brother, Grand General Liang Ji, held power, ushering in the peak of Liang consort clan domination.

v.t: Peasant uprising in Jiujiang
v.s: In Jiujiang Commandery (present-day Anhui and Jiangxi), a peasant uprising led by Xu Feng and Ma Mian broke out. The rebels wore yellow scarves, attacked county seats, and opened granaries, quickly growing to tens of thousands. Liang Ji mobilized troops from six commanderies, including Yangzhou and Xuzhou, and after months of fierce fighting, suppressed the revolt. However, small-scale uprisings continued to flare up, and the foundations of Han rule began to shake.

v.t: Emperor Chong of Han dies young; Emperor Zhi succeeds
v.s: Emperor Chong of Han died after only one year on the throne, at age three. Empress Dowager Liang and Liang Ji enthroned Liu Zuan, son of Liu Hong, Prince of Bohai, as Emperor Zhi of Han, aged eight. Liang Ji continued to monopolize power, and corruption worsened.

v.t: Peasant uprisings erupt one after another in various regions
v.s: Due to Liang Ji's tyranny and years of famine, numerous peasant uprisings broke out in Jiujiang, Lujiang, Danyang, and Kuaiji commanderies. Rebel groups ranged from several thousand to tens of thousands, attacking county seats and killing corrupt officials. Liang Ji mobilized large forces to suppress them, but the rebels used mountains and waterways to wage guerrilla warfare, repeatedly defeating government forces. These uprisings were a prelude to the later Yellow Turban Rebellion.

v.t: Liang Ji poisons Emperor Zhi of Han
v.s: Although young, Emperor Zhi was intelligent. At a court assembly, he pointed at Liang Ji in front of all the officials and said, 'This is a tyrannical general!' Liang Ji harbored a grudge, ordered his henchmen to poison the emperor's food, and killed him. The emperor was only nine years old. Liang Ji then enthroned Liu Zhi, Marquis of Liwu, as Emperor Huan of Han, aged fifteen. Liang Ji continued to hold power, more arrogant than ever.

v.t: The Pure Criticism (Qingyi) movement emerges among Taixue students
v.s: Taixue students in Luoyang, resentful of Liang Ji's tyranny, began gathering to discuss politics and evaluate officials. They praised upright officials like Li Gu and Du Qiao, and condemned Liang Ji and his cronies. The Qingyi movement gradually took shape, becoming an important form of resistance by the scholar-official class against eunuchs and consort clans.

v.t: Explicit record of Buddhism's official entry into the Central Plains
v.s: Emperor Huan of Han established a 'Futu Shrine' (Buddha shrine) in the palace to worship the Buddha – this is the earliest explicit official record of Buddhism spreading in the Central Plains. Emperor Huan himself followed Huang-Lao Daoism, regarding the Buddha as one of the immortals. This move promoted Buddhism's spread among the upper class and common people. Around this time, the Parthian monk An Shigao arrived in Luoyang and began translating scriptures. Buddhism thus began to take root in China.

v.t: Li Gu and Du Qiao are killed by Liang Ji
v.s: Grand Commandant Li Gu and Du Qiao, having opposed the enthronement of Liu Zhi as emperor, were falsely accused and imprisoned by Liang Ji. In prison, Li Gu wrote a defense, and his student Wang Diao and others protested his innocence. Fearing public opinion, Liang Ji released Li Gu. But soon after, he ordered Li and Du arrested and killed, their bodies displayed on the streets. Their deaths aroused widespread indignation among scholar-officials and Taixue students, and the anti-Liang Ji movement grew louder.

v.t: Christian writings increase in Rome
v.s: As Christianity spread within the Roman Empire, apologists such as Justin Martyr wrote many works defending the Christian faith and explaining its doctrines. Justin opened a school in Rome, teaching Christian philosophy and presenting Christianity as the 'highest philosophy.' The increase in such writings indicates accelerating missionary work among the educated classes.

v.t: Parthian monk An Shigao translates scriptures in China
v.s: An Shigao, a Parthian prince who renounced his throne to become a monk, arrived in Luoyang to translate Buddhist scriptures. He was fluent in Chinese, Sanskrit, and Parthian, and translated over thirty Hinayana scriptures, including the Anban Shouyi Jing (Sutra on Mindfulness of Breathing) and the Yin Chi Ru Jing (Sutra on the Entering of Consciousness). He systematically introduced Buddhist meditation and basic doctrines. An Shigao's work marked the beginning of systematic Buddhist translation in China, and he is revered as the pioneer of Chinese Buddhist scripture translation.

v.t: Liang Ji expands his mansion to extreme luxury
v.s: Grand General Liang Ji launched a massive construction project in Luoyang, expanding his mansion and gardens. His residence stretched for dozens of li, with pavilions, towers, rockeries, and ponds far exceeding the palace in luxury. Liang Ji also forcibly seized tens of thousands of hectares of farmland as his 'hunting ground', raising rabbits; anyone who trespassed was killed. Liang Ji's extravagance further intensified social conflict.

v.t: Rome celebrates the 900th anniversary of its founding
v.s: The Roman Empire held a grand celebration for the 900th anniversary of Rome's founding (traditional date 753 BC). Emperor Antoninus Pius presided over days of gladiatorial games, chariot races, and theatrical performances, distributing grain and money to the citizens. The festivities showcased the empire's power and cultural richness.

v.t: An Shigao continues translating scriptures and spreading Buddhism in Luoyang
v.s: The Parthian monk An Shigao continued translating scriptures and spreading Buddhist teachings in Luoyang. He not only translated texts but also explained meditation and doctrine to believers, teaching chanting in Sanskrit. He had several hundred disciples, including Lokaksema, who later became a famous translator. An Shigao also sent some Chinese Buddhist translations back to Parthia, promoting Buddhism in West Asia.

v.t: Greek medicine spreads widely in Rome
v.s: During the Roman Empire, the medical theories of the Greek physician Galen began to spread widely. Galen studied human anatomy through animal dissections, proposed the humoral theory and pneuma theory, and wrote numerous medical works. Roman aristocrats hired Greek doctors as personal physicians, and public hospitals began adopting Greek medical methods. The combination of Greek medicine and Roman public health improved the health of the Roman population.

v.t: Emperor Huan changes era name to Heping
v.s: Hoping to bring peace, Emperor Huan changed the era name to 'Heping' (Peace). But peace did not come; Liang Ji's tyranny continued, peasant revolts persisted, and the Eastern Han dynasty continued its decline amid darkness and turmoil.

v.t: Chinese papermaking spreads to foreign lands
