v.s: Cai Lun's improved papermaking spread from Luoyang to all commanderies and counties, replacing bamboo slips and silk, greatly promoting cultural dissemination. Meanwhile, paper was transmitted via the Silk Road to the Western Regions, Central Asia, and eventually to West Asia and Europe, revolutionizing world civilization. Papermaking is considered one of China's four great inventions.

v.t: Empress Dowager Liang dies
v.s: Empress Dowager Liang died of illness. On her deathbed, she left a will urging Emperor Huan to employ worthy ministers and reduce taxes. However, after losing the empress dowager as his protector, Liang Ji only became more domineering, not even respecting the emperor.

v.t: Ma Rong annotates Confucian classics
v.s: The Eastern Han classical scholar Ma Rong completed annotations on several Confucian classics, including the Classic of Filial Piety, the Analects, and the Book of Changes. He broke down the barriers between the New Text and Old Text schools, drawing on the strengths of both, forming a unique scholarly system. Ma Rong's commentaries were an important reference for Zheng Xuan, and they advanced the development of Confucian scholarship.

v.t: Emperor Huan of Han changes era name to Yuanjia
v.s: Emperor Huan changed the era name to 'Yuanjia' (Excellent Prosperity) and declared a general amnesty. However, Liang Ji's tyranny continued unabated, even more outrageous. Emperor Huan secretly began building strength to eliminate Liang Ji.

v.t: Parthian Empire recovers Mesopotamia
v.s: Taking advantage of Rome's preoccupation with border troubles, King Vologases III of Parthia led a western campaign and recaptured most of Mesopotamia, including the ancient cities of Nineveh and Babylon. The eastern Roman provinces were threatened, but Emperor Antoninus Pius chose a diplomatic solution, recognizing Parthian suzerainty over the region and avoiding a major war.

v.t: Cui Yuan composes the 'Zuo You Ming' (Inscription for a Seat)
v.s: The Eastern Han calligrapher and writer Cui Yuan composed the famous 'Zuo You Ming' (Inscription for a Seat), consisting of twenty lines, beginning with 'Do not speak of others' shortcomings, do not boast of your own.' It encouraged the author to be cautious in speech and action, tolerant of others. This work became an important model for later literati in self-cultivation and created the literary genre of the 'Zuo You Ming' (motto).

v.t: Zhu Mu memorializes to impeach Liang Ji
v.s: The Cishi (Inspector) of Jizhou, Zhu Mu, submitted a memorial to Emperor Huan impeaching Liang Ji and his cronies for corruption and oppressing the people. Zhu Mu wrote that 'Heaven has abandoned him; he cannot be restored,' urging Huan to resolve to eliminate Liang Ji. The memorial was intercepted by Liang Ji; Zhu Mu was arrested and imprisoned. Only after thousands of Taixue students led by Liu Tao gathered in protest was Zhu released.

v.t: Roman envoys reach China by sea
v.s: According to the Book of the Later Han, envoys from the Roman Empire (Daqin) reached Rinan Commandery (present-day central Vietnam) by sea and then traveled to Luoyang. This is one of the earliest official records of direct contact between China and the Roman Empire. Though the envoys may have been Roman merchants posing as diplomats, it reflects the desire of the two great empires to establish links.

v.t: The great classical scholar Zheng Xuan is born
v.s: The renowned Eastern Han classical scholar Zheng Xuan was born in Gaomi, Beihai (present-day Gaomi, Shandong). He later studied under Ma Rong and produced commentaries on all the Confucian classics, breaking down the barriers between New Text and Old Text schools, forming 'Zheng Studies' (Zheng Xue), which became the synthesis of Han dynasty classical learning. Zheng Xuan's interpretations dominated the Chinese explanation of Confucian classics for nearly two thousand years.

v.t: Yellow River floods catastrophically
v.s: The Yellow River burst its banks in Wei Commandery and Qinghe Commandery, flooding dozens of counties, drowning thousands of people, and leaving hundreds of thousands homeless. Emperor Huan ordered relief, opening granaries and conscripting laborers to repair the dikes. But the scale of the engineering was huge, the treasury was strained, and only patchwork repairs could be done, not fundamental solutions. Flooding became a major problem in the mid-to-late Eastern Han.

v.t: The Pure Criticism (Qingyi) movement grows among Taixue students
v.s: Taixue students in Luoyang, joined by local Confucian scholars, focused their 'Pure Criticism' on politics and eunuchs. They praised upright officials like Li Ying and Chen Fan as 'models of the empire', and satirized eunuchs as 'muddy currents'. The students also created lists such as 'Three Lords', 'Eight Excellencies', and 'Eight Gu's' to rank the conduct of scholar-officials. The Qingyi movement became an important form of struggle between the scholar-officials and eunuchs, and set the stage for the later 'Disasters of Partisan Prohibitions' (Danggu).

v.t: Peasant uprising in Mount Tai and Langye
v.s: A peasant uprising broke out in the Mount Tai and Langye areas, led by Gongsun Ju who proclaimed himself 'Emperor' and gathered tens of thousands to attack county seats. Emperor Huan mobilized forces from four provinces (Qing, Xu, Yan, Yu) to suppress it, taking more than a year of fierce fighting.

v.t: Xianbei leader Tanshihuai unifies the steppe
v.s: Xianbei chief Tanshihuai convened a tribal assembly at Mount Danhan (present-day Xinghe, Inner Mongolia), consolidating his absolute rule over all Xianbei tribes. He established three divisions (east, central, west), each under a commander, commanding over 100,000 cavalry. After unification, Tanshihuai repeatedly led his forces to raid the Eastern Han border, plundering people and property, becoming the most serious northern border threat to the Han.

v.t: Emperor Huan changes era name to Yongshou
v.s: Emperor Huan changed the era name to 'Yongshou' (Eternal Life), hoping to prolong the dynasty's existence. But the Eastern Han was already in dire straits: Liang Ji's tyranny, peasant uprisings, Xianbei incursions, and fiscal exhaustion. The era change brought no improvement.

v.t: Xianbei invade Eastern Han on a large scale
v.s: Tanshihuai led over 100,000 Xianbei cavalry in a massive three-pronged invasion of the Eastern Han: one route attacked Yunzhong Commandery, another attacked Wuyuan Commandery, and the third attacked Yanmen Commandery. Han forces retreated repeatedly, and thousands of border residents were captured. Emperor Huan ordered General Duliang Li Ying to resist; after fierce fighting, the Xianbei were repelled, but the border suffered heavy losses.

v.t: Spread of Christianity in Syria
v.s: The Syrian city of Antioch became an important base for the outward spread of Christianity. Missionaries set out from there to establish churches in Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome. The church at Antioch also organized large-scale missionary activities for the first time, laying the foundations for Christianity to become a world religion.

v.t: Peasant uprisings in Changsha and Lingling
v.s: Large-scale peasant uprisings broke out in Jingzhou commanderies such as Changsha, Lingling, and Wuling. The rebels attacked counties and killed corrupt officials, using mountains and rivers to wage guerrilla warfare against government forces for several years. Emperor Huan mobilized forces from Jingzhou and Jiaozhou to suppress them, which gradually succeeded.

v.t: Christian apologetic movement in Rome
v.s: The Christian apologist Justin Martyr wrote his First Apology in Rome, refuting charges of 'atheism', 'cannibalism', and 'incest' made against Christians, explaining Christian doctrine and worship, and appealing for tolerance from Emperor Antoninus Pius. Justin presented Christianity philosophically as the 'highest philosophy', seeking acceptance from the Roman elite.

v.t: Emperor Huan and eunuchs plot to eliminate Liang Ji
v.s: Unable to tolerate Liang Ji's tyranny any longer, Emperor Huan secretly contacted five eunuchs – Shan Chao, Ju Yuan, Tang Heng, Zuo Guan, Xu Huang – to plot the elimination of Liang Ji. Huan bit Shan Chao's arm, swearing blood brotherhood, and set a date for action. The eunuchs controlled part of the imperial guard, preparing a coup.

v.t: Gaul region accelerates Romanization
v.s: During the reign of Antoninus Pius, the province of Gaul (present-day France) rapidly Romanized. Latin became the official language of administration and commerce; Roman-style cities, roads, aqueducts, and theaters spread throughout Gaul. Gallic nobles entered the Roman Senate in large numbers, and Gaul became one of the most prosperous provinces of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Emperor Huan exterminates Liang Ji
v.s: Emperor Huan and the five eunuchs staged a coup, ordering the imperial guard to surround Liang Ji's mansion. Liang Ji and his wife Sun Shou committed suicide; hundreds of the Liang clan were executed. Liang Ji's property was confiscated, valued at over 3 billion coins, equivalent to more than a year's revenue for the Eastern Han. After killing Liang Ji, Huan enfeoffed the five eunuchs as the 'Five Marquises'. Eunuchs now came to power, and Eastern Han politics shifted from consort clan to eunuch domination.

v.t: Five eunuchs enfeoffed as marquises on the same day
v.s: To reward the five eunuchs (Shan Chao, Ju Yuan, Tang Heng, Zuo Guan, Xu Huang) for their role in eliminating Liang Ji, Emperor Huan on the same day enfeoffed all five as marquises, each with a fief of 10,000 households. Shan Chao was promoted to General of Chariots and Cavalry, controlling the imperial guards. The 'Five Marquises' wielded immense power, selling offices, living in luxury, and causing widespread outrage. Eunuch tyranny proved even darker than consort clan rule.

v.t: Rome launches a Parthian war
v.s: After the death of Emperor Antoninus Pius, the new co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus took office. As Parthia invaded Armenia, Rome declared war. Lucius Verus led the eastern campaign, part of the long-running Roman-Parthian conflict.

v.t: The 'Five Marquises' seize control of the government
v.s: After the elimination of Liang Ji, the eunuch 'Five Marquises' reached the height of their power. They not only controlled the government but also installed their cronies as provincial and commandery officials, even interfering in succession to the throne. Their relatives and servants bullied locals, seized land, and caused widespread resentment. Upright scholar-officials repeatedly impeached them, but Emperor Huan paid no heed.

v.t: Theater culture flourishes in the Roman Empire
v.s: Roman-style theaters were built across the Roman provinces, some seating tens of thousands. Often built on hillsides or using arch vaults, they hosted Greek tragedies, Roman comedies, mimes, acrobatics, and poetry recitations. Theater culture became an important part of daily entertainment, promoting the flourishing and dissemination of dramatic arts.

v.t: Antonine Plague breaks out
v.s: Roman troops returning from Mesopotamia brought back a virulent infectious disease (probably smallpox or measles). The plague spread rapidly through Rome and the provinces. At its peak, about 2,000 people died daily in Rome. The empire's total population may have fallen by a third, military strength severely weakened, and the economy devastated. This 'Antonine Plague' was a major turning point in the Roman Empire's decline.

v.t: Eunuch domination leads to extreme official corruption
v.s: After the 'Five Marquises' took power, the sale of offices became open and rampant. Their relatives and servants filled the provinces and commanderies, extorting bribes and oppressing the people. Upright officials were either marginalized or falsely imprisoned. The Eastern Han officialdom entered its darkest period; the people were destitute, and uprisings erupted everywhere.

v.t: Parthian Empire invades Armenia
v.s: Taking advantage of Rome's weakened state due to plague, King Vologases IV of Parthia invaded Armenia, deposed the pro-Roman king, and installed his own candidate. This triggered a new round of Roman-Parthian war, and Rome sent forces to rescue Armenia.

v.t: Li Ying appointed Colonel of the Capital (Sili Xiaowei), rectifies discipline
v.s: Li Ying was appointed Colonel of the Capital (Sili Xiaowei), responsible for supervising the capital and its environs. He dared to offend the powerful, strictly punishing lawless eunuch relatives and corrupt officials, even arresting and punishing relatives of the 'Five Marquises'. His uprightness won him respect from scholar-officials and commoners; the Taixue students called him a 'model for the empire'. However, this also set the stage for intense conflict with eunuchs.

v.t: Roman Empire prepares for eastern campaign
v.s: Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus actively prepared for war against Parthia. Rome massed about 70,000 troops in the east, including elite legions transferred from the Danube and Rhine. Lucius Verus was nominally in command, but actual control fell to experienced generals.

v.t: Zhu Mu writes the 'Chong Hou Lun' (On Esteeming Honesty)
v.s: The Eastern Han scholar Zhu Mu wrote the 'Chong Hou Lun' (On Esteeming Honesty), criticizing the contemporary 'shallow customs'. He advocated a return to simple, honest, and generous moral standards, opposing hypocrisy, fraud, and extravagance. The essay represented the scholar-officials' anxiety about declining social morality and reflected the Confucian idea of 'virtue carries things'.

v.t: Emperor Huan tours Jingzhou
v.s: Emperor Huan, accompanied by eunuchs and favorites, made a southern tour of Jingzhou and Wancheng, ostensibly to inspect but actually for pleasure. He spent extravagantly on hunting, drinking, and entertainment, ignoring advice from upright officials. Local officials extorted the people to provide for the emperor, causing widespread resentment.

v.t: Rome captures Armenia
v.s: The Roman eastern army, under the command of Statius Priscus, governor of Cappadocia, invaded Armenia, defeated the Parthian army, and recovered all of Armenia. Rome reestablished Armenia as a protectorate and installed a pro-Roman king.

v.t: On the eve of the first 'Disaster of Partisan Prohibitions'
v.s: The conflict between eunuchs and scholar-officials reached a boiling point. The eunuchs falsely accused Li Ying, Chen Fan, and other scholar-officials of forming a faction and slandering the court. They demanded that Emperor Huan suppress them. Though hesitant, Huan, under pressure from eunuchs, began to prepare arrests. The first 'Disaster of Partisan Prohibitions' was imminent.

v.t: Crisis on the Roman Empire's Germanic border
v.s: Germanic tribes, especially the Marcomanni and Quadi, crossed the Danube and invaded the Roman province of Pannonia. They destroyed cities, massacred residents, and even threatened Italy itself. Emperor Marcus Aurelius had to leave the eastern front and personally command the defenses on the Danube, marking the beginning of a full-scale crisis on the northern frontier.

v.t: First 'Disaster of Partisan Prohibitions' erupts
v.s: The eunuch faction falsely accused hundreds of scholar-officials, including Li Ying, Chen Fan, and Fan Pang, of 'slandering the court and forming cliques.' Emperor Huan ordered the arrest of the 'partisans'. Li Ying and others were imprisoned. Over 20,000 Taixue students gathered in protest, demanding their release. Under public pressure, Huan released them but permanently banned them from holding office, in what is known as the 'Disaster of Partisan Prohibitions' (Danggu). This was the first major defeat of the scholar-officials in their struggle against eunuchs, and marked the further darkening of Eastern Han politics.

v.t: Roman envoys reach China by sea
v.s: According to the Book of the Later Han, envoys sent by Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius reached Rinan Commandery (present-day central Vietnam) via the Indian Ocean, then traveled to Luoyang to pay homage to Emperor Huan. They presented exotic products such as ivory, rhino horn, and hawksbill turtles. This marks the first official contact between the two great empires. Though the envoys may have been Roman merchants posing, it reflects the desire for direct contact between Rome and Han China.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius defeats the Marcomanni
v.s: Emperor Marcus Aurelius led Roman forces to a victory over the Marcomanni coalition in Pannonia, temporarily halting the Germanic southward advance. However, plague and manpower shortages prevented a complete victory; a treaty was signed allowing Marcomanni to settle along the border in exchange for not attacking Roman territory.

v.t: Emperor Huan pardons the 'partisans'
v.s: At the urging of Empress Dowager Dou, Emperor Huan pardoned Li Ying and other 'partisans', releasing them to their homes. However, they remained permanently banned from holding office. The struggle between scholar-officials and eunuchs temporarily subsided.

v.t: Emperor Huan of Han dies
v.s: Emperor Huan of Han died of illness in Luoyang at age 36. Without an heir, Empress Dowager Dou and her father Dou Wu enthroned Liu Hong, Marquis of Jieduting, as Emperor Ling of Han, aged twelve. Empress Dowager Dou served as regent; Dou Wu was appointed Grand General, and together with Chen Fan, they began to purge eunuch influence.

v.t: Dou Wu and Chen Fan fail in plot to kill eunuchs
v.s: Grand General Dou Wu and Grand Tutor Chen Fan secretly plotted to eliminate eunuch forces, planning a coup with the help of imperial guard officers. However, eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu got wind of it and acted first, seizing Emperor Ling and Empress Dowager Dou, issuing a forged edict to kill Dou Wu and Chen Fan and their relatives, and executing more than a hundred of their disciples. Empress Dowager Dou was placed under house arrest, and eunuchs took full control.

v.t: Eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu take power
v.s: After killing Dou Wu and Chen Fan, eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu seized control of the government. Cao Jie was promoted to Colonel of the Changle Guard and enfeoffed as marquis; Wang Fu was appointed Palace Attendant. Eunuch power reached its zenith in the Eastern Han, as they could appoint and dismiss officials, interfere with justice, and even decide the succession.

v.t: Second 'Disaster of Partisan Prohibitions'
v.s: Eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu accused Li Ying, Du Mi, and others of plotting against the state, and persuaded Emperor Ling to order a massive roundup of 'partisans'. Over a hundred scholar-officials, including Li Ying, were executed; 600-700 more were permanently banned from office, and their families were exiled. Taixue students were forbidden to gather, and the Pure Criticism movement was suppressed. The second Danggu was more brutal than the first, dealing a devastating blow to the Eastern Han scholar-official class.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius concludes a treaty with Germanic tribes
v.s: Emperor Marcus Aurelius made a treaty with the Marcomanni, Quadi, and other Germanic tribes, allowing them to settle within Roman borders and provide troops as allied forces. This was the first large-scale Roman authorization of barbarian settlement within the empire, setting a precedent for later barbarian invasions.

v.t: Eunuchs wield immense power
v.s: Eunuchs Cao Jie, Wang Fu, Zhang Rang, Zhao Zhong – ten in total – controlled the government. Emperor Ling called Zhang Rang 'Father' and Zhao Zhong 'Mother'. The eunuchs sold offices, extorted people, and their relatives and servants ran rampant. A folk rhyme in the capital said: 'You recommend a scholar – he doesn't know the books; you recommend a filial son – he lives apart from his dad. The pure and poor are muddy like mud; the high-ranked generals are cowardly like chicks.'

v.t: Duan Ying pacifies the Western Qiang
v.s: The Eastern Han general Duan Ying launched a final offensive against the Western Qiang, defeating them decisively at Fengyi Mountain (in present-day Gansu), killing over 8,000. He then relentlessly pursued and eliminated the remaining forces of the Shaodang and Zhong Qiang tribes. The Western Qiang rebellion was thus basically pacified, but the Eastern Han's national power was largely exhausted.

v.t: Emperor Ling performs capping ceremony and declares general amnesty
v.s: Emperor Ling of Han, now 15 years old, held his coming-of-age ceremony (capping) and assumed formal rule. To celebrate, he declared a general amnesty, promoted eunuchs Zhang Rang and Zhao Zhong to marquises, and granted them large estates and slaves. Not only did Emperor Ling fail to control the eunuchs, he became even more dependent on them.

v.t: Empress Song is deposed
v.s: Believing slanders from eunuchs, Emperor Ling deposed Empress Song, imprisoning her in the palace. She soon died, and her relatives were executed or exiled. Eunuch influence had reached the point of determining the appointment and deposition of the empress.

v.t: Emperor Ling changes era name to Xiping
v.s: Emperor Ling changed the era name to 'Xiping' (Great Peace), and declared a general amnesty. But the change did not reverse the Eastern Han's decline; eunuch tyranny, peasant uprisings, and border incursions remained severe.

v.t: Xianbei border raids intensify
v.s: Xianbei leader Tanshihuai led continuous raids on the Han's Youzhou and Bingzhou borders. Emperor Ling sent Colonel Protecting the Wuhuan (Hu Wuhuan Xiaowei) Xia Yu to counterattack, but Tanshihuai defeated him decisively; Xia Yu barely escaped with his life. Thereafter, the Xianbei reached their peak, controlling the entire Mongolian plateau and becoming the most serious northern border threat to the Eastern Han.

v.t: Earthquake in Luoyang
v.s: A strong earthquake struck the capital Luoyang, collapsing countless palaces, offices, and homes, killing or injuring hundreds. The earthquake was seen as a warning from heaven against eunuch misrule, and Taixue students and scholar-officials petitioned for the removal of eunuchs. Emperor Ling ignored them and instead suppressed the petitioners.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius launches war against the Quadi
v.s: The Quadi violated their treaty with Rome and again invaded Pannonia. Emperor Marcus Aurelius led a large army on a campaign, defeating the Quadi on the Danube and forcing them to sue for peace again. This war was part of Aurelius' long-term Marcomannic Wars.

v.t: Duan Ying appointed Grand Commandant
v.s: Duan Ying, who had pacified the Western Qiang, was appointed Grand Commandant by Emperor Ling. Duan was an honest official, but because he came from a poor family, he was shunned by the scholar-officials. The eunuchs exploited this conflict to win him over; Duan gradually sided with the eunuchs and became their accomplice.

v.t: Xianbei leader Tanshihuai dies
v.s: Xianbei leader Tanshihuai died, and his son Helian succeeded. Helian was mediocre and unable to control the tribes. The Xianbei empire began to fragment, though its various tribes still frequently raided the Eastern Han border.

v.t: Xiping Stone Classics are erected
v.s: Cai Yong and others petitioned Emperor Ling to engrave the Confucian classics on stone tablets and set them up outside the Taixue (Imperial University) as the standard versions. The project carved seven classics (Book of Changes, Book of Documents, Book of Songs, Book of Rites, Spring and Autumn Annals, Gongyang Commentary, and Analects). It took eight years, producing 46 stone tablets, each about one zhang high. The Xiping Stone Classics are the earliest officially established Confucian canonical text in Chinese history, playing an important role in standardizing classics and promoting Confucian learning.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius tours the East
v.s: After pacifying the Germanic border, Emperor Marcus Aurelius toured the eastern provinces, visiting Athens, Antioch, and Alexandria. He established several philosophy chairs in Athens, subsidizing scholars and promoting a revival of Greek culture.

v.t: Cai Yong memorializes against eunuchs
v.s: The Court Gentleman (Yilang) Cai Yong submitted a memorial to Emperor Ling exposing the corruption of eunuchs and demanding their removal and the appointment of worthy ministers. The eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu, enraged, falsely accused Cai Yong of slandering the court and had him imprisoned. Cai was sentenced to death, but the sentence was commuted to exile in Shuofang Commandery.

v.t: Xianbei raid Youzhou annually
v.s: Led by Helian, the Xianbei tribes raided the Han's Youzhou each year, plundering people, livestock, and grain, bringing great suffering. Emperor Ling dispatched troops to defend, but due to corruption and insufficient pay, the border forces were ineffective and suffered repeated defeats.

v.t: Eastern Han's large expedition against Xianbei ends in defeat
v.s: Emperor Ling ordered Colonel Protecting the Wuhuan Xia Yu, General of the Break-the-Xianbei (Po Xianbei Zhonglang Jiang) Tian Yan, and Colonel of the Xiongnu (Xiongnu Zhonglang Jiang) Zang Min to lead three armies in a large expedition to destroy the Xianbei. However, Helian concentrated his forces to meet each separately, and the Han armies were heavily defeated. Xia Yu and Tian Yan narrowly escaped with their lives, losing tens of thousands of soldiers. This was the biggest Han defeat in its wars against the Xianbei.

v.t: Christians are persecuted in Lyon
v.s: A large-scale persecution of Christians occurred in the city of Lyon in the Roman province of Gaul. Influenced by rumors that Christians engaged in 'cannibalism and incest', the local populace arrested and tortured them. Many were sentenced to death, either fed to wild animals or beheaded. Bishop Pothinus of Lyon, over 90 years old, was martyred. This persecution was part of the social unrest following the Antonine Plague, reflecting the difficult situation of Christianity in the Roman Empire.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius writes Meditations
v.s: While campaigning on the Germanic front, Emperor Marcus Aurelius wrote the twelve books of Meditations in Greek. This philosophical work is a self-dialogue and moral reflection by the emperor, setting forth the core ideas of Stoic philosophy: the universe is governed by reason (logos), humans should follow nature, control desires, and perform their duties. Meditations is still considered a classic of Western philosophy, and is one of the few philosophical works left by an ancient ruler.

v.t: Emperor Ling sets up an office to sell official posts in the Western Garden
v.s: To finance his extravagant lifestyle and palace construction, Emperor Ling opened a 'market' in the Western Garden (Xi Yuan) to sell official positions. The price for the Three Excellences was 10 million coins; the Nine Ministers, 5 million; county magistrates, set by size of county. Officials could pay in installments and then squeeze the people after taking office. This caused the complete collapse of the Han bureaucracy and further burdened the people.

v.t: Eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu manipulate the government
v.s: Eunuchs Cao Jie, Wang Fu, Zhang Rang, Zhao Zhong, and others dominated the court, selling offices and forming cliques. Emperor Ling called Zhang Rang 'Father' and followed his every word. Upright officials who voiced even mild dissent were falsely imprisoned. Eastern Han politics reached its darkest period.

v.t: Wang Fu is executed; eunuch infighting
v.s: Infighting among eunuchs over power led to conflict. Eunuch Cao Jie, in collusion with Zhang Rang, accused fellow eunuch Wang Fu of corruption. Emperor Ling ordered Wang Fu's execution. The infighting somewhat weakened the eunuch clique, but did not end eunuch domination.

v.t: Roman public health advances
v.s: The Roman Empire built numerous public baths, aqueducts, and sewers. Rome alone had 11 major aqueducts and hundreds of public baths, with a daily water supply of over one billion liters. This system reduced disease transmission and increased the average life expectancy of Romans. The Roman public health system was unparalleled in the ancient world.

v.t: Marcus Aurelius dies; Commodus succeeds
v.s: Emperor Marcus Aurelius died of illness on the Germanic front at age 59. His son Commodus succeeded. Commodus immediately abandoned his father's philosophical spirit and military policies, made peace with the Germanic tribes, and quickly returned to Rome. Commodus' rule marked the end of the 'Five Good Emperors' golden age, and the beginning of tyranny and disorder.

v.t: Galen's medical system reaches culmination
v.s: The Roman/Greek physician Claudius Galen completed his medical system, with groundbreaking achievements in anatomy, physiology, and pharmacology. Through animal dissection, he identified the concept of blood circulation (though not complete), distinguished sensory and motor nerves, and described heart valves. Galen wrote about 500 medical treatises, and his medical system dominated Europe and the Middle East for over 1300 years.

v.t: Emperor Ling establishes Empress He
v.s: Emperor Ling elevated Consort He to Empress. Empress He came from a butcher's family, and her brother He Jin thus entered officialdom. Empress He bore Liu Bian, who was later made crown prince. The He Jin family later became an important consort clan in the Eastern Han.

v.t: Zhang Jiao founds the Way of Supreme Peace (Taiping Dao)
v.s: Zhang Jiao from Julu, calling himself 'Great Worthy Master' (Daxian Liangshi), founded the Way of Supreme Peace (Taiping Dao). He used talismanic water and incantations to heal the sick, and recruited many disciples. Over ten years, his followers reached hundreds of thousands, spread across eight provinces: Qing, Xu, You, Ji, Jing, Yang, Yan, and Yu. Zhang Jiao proclaimed the slogan: 'The azure sky is dead; the yellow sky will rise; in the year jiazi, the world will be blessed.' He secretly prepared for an uprising.

v.t: Roman forts in Britain are built
v.s: Emperor Commodus ordered the construction of a series of forts in northern Britain, strengthening the defenses of Hadrian's Wall. These forts were garrisoned by auxiliary troops, monitoring Pict activity and protecting Romanized southern Britain.

v.t: Zhang Jiao organizes thirty-six 'Fangs' (regions)
v.s: Zhang Jiao divided the Way of Supreme Peace followers into thirty-six 'Fangs' (regions). The larger Fangs had over 10,000 followers, the smaller 6-7,000. Each Fang had a leader called Qu Shuai. Zhang Jiao's brothers Zhang Bao and Zhang Liang served as Qu Shuai of the large Fangs, directing followers across the country. The Way of Supreme Peace had in fact formed a tightly organized military structure, ready to revolt at any time.

v.t: Commodus' tyrannical rule intensifies
v.s: Emperor Commodus became increasingly cruel and paranoid, executing many senators and generals, including his own sister Lucilla. He neglected state affairs, obsessively participated in gladiatorial combats, and styled himself the 'Roman Hercules'. Commodus' tyranny caused widespread discontent and unrest throughout the empire.

v.t: Yellow Turban Rebellion imminent
v.s: Zhang Jiao ordered all Qu Shuai to rise in revolt simultaneously on the fifth day of the third month of the jiazi year (184). He sent his disciple Tang Zhou to Luoyang to contact the eunuch Feng Xu as an inside agent. Way of Supreme Peace followers were spread throughout Luoyang; the rebellion was imminent.

v.t: The Colosseum holds grand games
v.s: Emperor Commodus presided over lavish gladiatorial games and animal hunts at the Colosseum. He personally fought wild animals and gladiators, killing hundreds of lions and dozens of gladiators. The Roman populace numbly watched bloodthirsty entertainment as the empire declined.

v.t: The Yellow Turban Rebellion breaks out
v.s: Zhang Jiao's disciple Tang Zhou betrayed the plot and informed the authorities in Luoyang. Emperor Ling ordered the arrest of Ma Yuanyi, who was executed by dismemberment, and a frantic search for Way of Supreme Peace believers. Zhang Jiao was forced to revolt early, proclaiming himself 'General of Heaven', Zhang Bao 'General of Earth', and Zhang Liang 'General of Man'. The rebels wore yellow scarves, thus they were called the 'Yellow Turbans'. The rebellion quickly swept through eight provinces, spreading alarm throughout the court.

v.t: Eastern Han lifts the Partisan Prohibitions to fight the Yellow Turbans
v.s: The Yellow Turban rebellion was so fierce that the Han court could not suppress it. Acting on the advice of Huangfu Song, Emperor Ling lifted the Partisan Prohibitions, pardoning the banned scholar-officials and allowing them to serve again to organize forces to suppress the rebellion. The 'partisans' came forth to help, making a last effort to save the Eastern Han.

v.t: Huangfu Song and Zhu Jun defeat the Yellow Turbans
v.s: The Eastern Han appointed Huangfu Song, Zhu Jun, and Lu Zhi as principal generals to suppress the Yellow Turbans. Huangfu Song used fire to defeat Bo Cai's Yellow Turban army at Changshe, killing tens of thousands. Zhu Jun defeated Zhang Mancheng's force at Wancheng. Lu Zhi besieged Zhang Jiao's main force at Guangzong (present-day Weixian, Hebei). The Yellow Turbans suffered heavy losses and retreated to fortified positions.

v.t: Zhang Jiao dies; Yellow Turbans collapse
v.s: Yellow Turban leader Zhang Jiao died during the battle of Guangzong. Huangfu Song launched a final assault, defeating Zhang Liang and Zhang Bao; Zhang Liang was killed, Zhang Bao captured and executed. The main Yellow Turban forces were destroyed; remnants retreated to the mountains to continue fighting. The great Yellow Turban Rebellion was defeated, but the Eastern Han was severely weakened by the suppression, and regional warlords began to rise.

v.t: Rebellion of Bian Zhang and Han Sui in Liangzhou
v.s: Though the Yellow Turbans were suppressed, the foundations of Han rule were shaken. A rebellion led by Bian Zhang and Han Sui broke out in Liangzhou. They allied with the Qiang, raising an army of over 100,000, raiding Longxi and Hanyang, and killing the Han Colonel Protecting the Qiang. The Liangzhou rebellion lasted for years, becoming a major border problem in the late Eastern Han.

v.t: Black Mountain Bandits uprising
v.s: Yellow Turban remnants led by Zhang Yan gathered at Black Mountain in Jizhou (present-day Xunxian, Henan), calling themselves the 'Black Mountain Bandits', claiming to have over one million followers. They waged guerrilla warfare against government forces and became the most powerful rebel force in the north.

v.t: Roman government faces financial difficulties
v.s: Emperor Commodus's extravagance and years of war led to severe financial strain. The government heavily debased the currency, reducing the silver content of the denarius, causing inflation. Economic decline worsened social tensions, and the empire entered the eve of a general crisis.

v.t: Han Sui kills Bian Zhang, takes sole command
v.s: Infighting split the Liangzhou rebels; Han Sui killed Bian Zhang and absorbed his followers, becoming the sole leader of the Liangzhou rebels. Together with Ma Teng, Han Sui controlled most of Liangzhou, defying the Han court.

v.t: Emperor Ling builds the Bi Gui Yuan pleasure park
v.s: For his amusement, Emperor Ling built the Bi Gui Yuan garden in the Western Garden, imitating a marketplace with palace ladies playing merchants while he played a wealthy merchant. He also built the 'Naked Bathing Hall', forcing ladies to swim naked, sinking into debauchery. The Eastern Han government had reached the depths of corruption.

v.t: Zhang Chun and Zhang Ju rebel in Youzhou
v.s: Zhang Chun and Zhang Ju from Fanyang Commandery, together with Wuhuan chieftain Qiuliju, launched a rebellion, killing the Han Colonel Protecting the Wuhuan and the Grand Administrator of Youbeiping. Zhang Ju proclaimed himself 'Emperor', Zhang Chun called himself 'General of Heaven-Bridging, Prince of Anding'. They led over 100,000 followers, raiding Youzhou and Jizhou.

v.t: Cao Cao appointed Chancellor of Jinan
v.s: Cao Cao was appointed Chancellor of Jinan by Emperor Ling. He vigorously reformed the administration, dismissing eight corrupt county magistrates and banning improper sacrifices, bringing order to Jinan. However, because he offended local strongmen and eunuchs, Cao Cao was soon transferred.

v.t: Eastern Han changes provincial inspectors (Cishi) to governors (Zhou Mu)
v.s: To suppress the numerous uprisings, Emperor Ling, following Liu Yan's advice, changed the position of provincial inspector (Cishi) to governor (Zhou Mu), giving them full military, fiscal, and civil authority over their provinces. This reform temporarily strengthened the suppression of uprisings, but led to the growth of regional warlords, rendering the central government powerless in name only.

v.t: Establishment of the Eight Colonels of the Western Garden
v.s: To strengthen central forces, Emperor Ling established a new army in the Western Garden, commanded by eight colonels. Eunuch Jian Shuo was appointed Colonel of the Superior Army, commanding all. Cao Cao was appointed Colonel of the Model Army, and Yuan Shao Colonel of the Central Army. The new Western Garden army became the last central elite force of the Eastern Han.

v.t: Emperor Ling of Han dies; Emperor Shao succeeds
v.s: Emperor Ling of Han died of illness in Luoyang at age 34. Grand General He Jin enthroned Crown Prince Liu Bian as Emperor Shao of Han. Empress Dowager He served as regent, and He Jin took full control. Eunuch Jian Shuo attempted to change the succession but was killed by He Jin.

v.t: He Jin's plot against eunuchs fails; he is killed
v.s: Grand General He Jin and Colonel of the Capital (Sili Xiaowei) Yuan Shao plotted to kill the eunuchs, but Empress Dowager He hesitated. On Yuan Shao's advice, He Jin summoned regional warlords like Dong Zhuo to march on the capital to pressure the empress dowager. Eunuchs Zhang Rang and Duan Gui got wind of it and struck first, killing He Jin inside the palace. He Jin's subordinates Wu Kuang and Yuan Shao then stormed the palace, slaughtering over 2,000 eunuchs.

v.t: Dong Zhuo enters Luoyang and usurps power
v.s: The Governor of Bingzhou, Dong Zhuo, led his Liangzhou army into Luoyang, absorbed the forces of He Jin and Ding Yuan, and took control of the court. Dong Zhuo deposed Emperor Shao and enthroned his brother Liu Xie as Emperor Xian of Han, appointing himself Chancellor of State (Xiangguo) and monopolizing power. Dong Zhuo was brutal and lawless, allowing his troops to loot, throwing Luoyang into chaos.

v.t: Commodus is assassinated
v.s: Emperor Commodus's tyranny led to a palace coup. His mistress Marcia and Praetorian Prefect Laetus conspired to strangle Commodus while he bathed. Commodus's death ended the Nerva-Antonine dynasty, and the Roman Empire soon descended into civil war, beginning the 'Crisis of the Third Century'.

v.t: Christianity spreads rapidly in Egypt
v.s: Christianity grew rapidly in Alexandria, Egypt, establishing a famous catechetical school that produced great Church Fathers like Origen. Egyptian Christians translated the Bible into Coptic, forming a distinct Egyptian Christian tradition (the Coptic Church).

v.t: Lords of Guandong form coalition against Dong Zhuo
v.s: Cao Cao, Yuan Shao, Yuan Shu, Liu Dai, Wang Kuang, and other provincial warlords from east of Hangu Pass formed a coalition against Dong Zhuo, electing Yuan Shao as leader. The coalition, claiming 100,000 troops, advanced on Luoyang. Fearful, Dong Zhuo decided to move the capital to Chang'an.

v.t: Dong Zhuo forces Emperor Xian to move the capital to Chang'an
v.s: Dong Zhuo forced Emperor Xian and the court officials to relocate to Chang'an, setting fire to Luoyang, burning palaces, government offices, and homes, turning an area 200 li around the capital into ashes. Dong Zhuo also dug up imperial tombs, looted treasures, and forced the population westward; countless died on the way.

v.t: Sun Jian enters Luoyang and recovers the Imperial Seal
v.s: Sun Jian, Grand Administrator of Changsha, led his forces into Luoyang, driving out Dong Zhuo's general Lu Bu, and recovering the ruined capital. In a well in the southern part of the city, Sun Jian discovered the Imperial Seal (Chuan Guo Xi). He kept it for himself. This event later provided the Sun Wu regime with a symbol of legitimacy.

v.t: Rome enters the Crisis of the Third Century
v.s: After Commodus's death, the Roman Empire fell into civil war. Within a single year, five men claimed the throne (Pertinax, Julianus, Niger, Albinus, Severus), known as the 'Year of the Five Emperors'. This marked the beginning of the Third Century Crisis, a fifty-year period of military anarchy.

v.t: Yuan Shao seizes Ji Province
v.s: Yuan Shao, following adviser Feng Ji's plan, allied with Gongsun Zan to attack Han Fu, Governor of Ji Province, while sending Gao Gan and Xun Chen to persuade Han Fu to surrender. Fearful, Han Fu ceded Ji Province to Yuan Shao. Yuan Shao then appointed himself Governor of Ji Province, greatly increasing his power and becoming the strongest warlord in the north.

v.t: Cao Cao incorporates Qingzhou Yellow Turbans into his forces
v.s: Cao Cao defeated a 300,000-strong Qingzhou Yellow Turban army in Yan Province and incorporated their elite into his own forces, called the 'Qingzhou Corps'. This force became Cao Cao's foundation; he established himself in Yan Province and began his quest for control of the Central Plains.

v.t: Wang Yun plots and kills Dong Zhuo
v.s: Minister Wang Yun exploited the conflict between Dong Zhuo and his adopted son Lu Bu, driving a wedge between them. Wang Yun and Lu Bu assassinated Dong Zhuo in front of the Weiyang Palace, then executed his clan. After Dong Zhuo's death, the people of Chang'an danced and sang, burning Dong Zhuo's fat as a lamp, calling it 'Heaven's lamp'.

v.t: Li Jue and Guo Si counterattack Chang'an
v.s: Dong Zhuo's former generals Li Jue and Guo Si, acting on Jia Xu's advice, led their Liangzhou army in a counterattack on Chang'an. Lu Bu was defeated and fled. Li Jue and Guo Si entered Chang'an, killed Wang Yun, and took control of Emperor Xian. The Guanzhong region descended into chaos, with hundreds of thousands dying in the fighting.

v.t: Cao Cao defeats remaining Yellow Turbans and occupies Yan Province
v.s: In Yan Province, Cao Cao defeated the remaining Yellow Turbans, incorporating 300,000 surrendering troops, greatly increasing his strength. Local elites recommended Cao Cao as Governor of Yan Province. Cao Cao began to build his own base, laying the foundation for his eventual unification of the north.

v.t: Septimius Severus becomes emperor
v.s: The governor of Pannonia, Septimius Severus, marched on Rome, defeated the Praetorian Guard, and killed Emperor Julianus. The Senate was forced to recognize him as emperor. Severus founded the Severan dynasty, temporarily stabilizing Rome, but his reliance on the military inaugurated the age of the 'soldier-emperors'.

v.t: Cao Cao attacks Xuzhou, commits massacres
v.s: To avenge his father Cao Song (killed by subordinates of Tao Qian, Governor of Xuzhou), Cao Cao led an army to attack Xuzhou, slaughtering hundreds of thousands of civilians along the way. It was said that the Si River was blocked by corpses. Cao Cao's atrocities caused widespread outrage; Chen Gong, Zhang Miao, and others rebelled against Cao Cao in Yan Province, allying with Lü Bu.

v.t: Severe famine in Chang'an
v.s: Due to war and natural disasters, the Chang'an region controlled by Li Jue and Guo Si suffered a severe famine. Grain prices skyrocketed, and cannibalism occurred. Emperor Xian ordered the opening of granaries, but the grain was stolen by Li Jue and Guo Si's troops, and countless people starved to death.

v.t: Drought and locusts devastate the Three Auxiliaries
v.s: The Three Auxiliaries region around Chang'an suffered years of drought; locusts darkened the sky, and crops failed entirely. People ate wild herbs and bark, even resorting to cannibalism. Emperor Xian himself ate beans boiled with vegetables. The famine reduced Chang'an's population by two-thirds.

v.t: Liu Bei aids Xuzhou and begins to make his mark
v.s: Tao Qian, Governor of Xuzhou, was besieged by Cao Cao and sought help from Tian Kai, Inspector of Qingzhou. Tian Kai sent Liu Bei with his troops to Xuzhou. Liu Bei had only about 1,000 men and a few thousand Xianbei cavalry, but he fought bravely, earning great fame. Tao Qian gave Liu Bei 4,000 Danyang soldiers, strengthening his forces.

v.t: Li Jue and Guo Si fight each other; Emperor Xian flees east
v.s: Li Jue and Guo Si fell out over power, fighting within Chang'an, killing tens of thousands. Emperor Xian, protected by Dong Cheng and Yang Feng, fled Chang'an and headed east to Luoyang. The journey was arduous; many officials died on the road.

v.t: Sun Ce emerges in Jiangdong
v.s: Sun Ce, using the Imperial Seal his father Sun Jian had recovered as collateral, borrowed 3,000 troops from Yuan Shu, crossed the Yangtze, and defeated Liu Yao, Yan Baihu, and others, occupying Kuaiji and Wu commanderies. Sun Ce knew how to use people and maintained strict military discipline; many heroes of Jiangdong rallied to him, laying the foundation for his younger brother Sun Quan's later establishment of the state of Wu.

v.t: Severus defeats his eastern rival
v.s: Emperor Severus led an army east, defeating his rival Niger at the Battle of Issus, then advanced into Mesopotamia, capturing Niger's capital, Antioch. Severus executed Niger and his supporters, consolidating his control over the eastern part of the empire.

v.t: Cao Cao welcomes Emperor Xian and moves the capital to Xuchang
v.s: Cao Cao, acting on Xun Yu's advice, led his army into Luoyang, welcomed Emperor Xian, and moved the capital to Xuchang. Cao Cao appointed himself Grand General (later ceded to Yuan Shao) and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Wuping, controlling the court. 'Using the emperor to command the lords' (Xie tianzi yi ling zhuhou) gave him an absolute political advantage.

v.t: Cao Cao implements the Tuntian (military-agricultural colony) system in Xuchang
v.s: Following the advice of Zao Zhi and Han Hao, Cao Cao recruited refugees near Xuchang to implement 'tuntian' (state-managed military-agricultural colonies). The state provided land, oxen, and seeds, and the harvest was split between the state and the settlers. The tuntian system solved Cao Cao's grain supply problem, attracted many refugees, and became the economic foundation of the Cao Wei regime.

v.t: Yuan Shu proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Yuan Shu proclaimed himself emperor at Shouchun, establishing the state of Zhong (Zhong Shi). He called himself 'Emperor of Zhong', appointed officials, and established a court. This act of usurpation provoked opposition from all the lords; Cao Cao, Liu Bei, Sun Ce, and others formed a coalition against Yuan Shu, who was soon isolated and declined rapidly.

v.t: Cao Cao campaigns against Zhang Xiu
v.s: Cao Cao led an army against Zhang Xiu, who surrendered. However, Cao Cao then took Zhang Xiu's aunt as a concubine. Zhang Xiu, enraged, suddenly rebelled and attacked Cao Cao's camp. Cao Cao's eldest son Cao Ang, his nephew Cao Anmin, and his general Dian Wei were killed. Cao Cao barely escaped with his life. This was a costly defeat for Cao Cao.

v.t: Cao Cao kills Lü Bu
v.s: Cao Cao led his army to besiege Xiapi (present-day Suining, Jiangsu) and captured and executed Lü Bu and his generals Chen Gong and Gao Shun. Lü Bu, known for his treachery, ultimately brought about his own end. Cao Cao accepted the surrender of Zhang Liao and others, incorporating Xuzhou into his domains.

v.t: Sun Ce unifies Jiangdong
v.s: Sun Ce captured Kuaiji, Wu, Danyang, and other commanderies, defeated separatist forces like Yan Baihu and Xu Gong, and recruited renowned generals like Taishi Ci, largely unifying the six commanderies of Jiangdong. Sun Ce was appointed General Who Subdues Rebels (Tao Ni Jiangjun) and Marquis of Wu by the court, laying the foundation for the Eastern Wu state.

v.t: Severus captures the Parthian capital Ctesiphon
v.s: Emperor Severus launched a war against Parthia, capturing its capital Ctesiphon and seizing enormous wealth. He annexed northern Mesopotamia as a new province, restoring Roman hegemony in the region.

v.t: Yuan Shao defeats Gongsun Zan and unifies Hebei
v.s: After a three-year siege of Yi Fortress (present-day Xiongxian, Hebei), Yuan Shao's forces broke through Gongsun Zan's fortress. Gongsun Zan committed suicide, and his troops were absorbed by Yuan Shao. Yuan Shao now held You, Ji, Qing, and Bing provinces, commanding an army of 100,000, becoming the most powerful warlord in the north.

v.t: Hua Tuo practices medicine and saves lives
v.s: The famous late Eastern Han physician Hua Tuo traveled widely, skilled in surgery. He invented an anesthetic called 'mafeisan' (literally 'cannabis boiling powder'), the world's earliest general anesthetic, and could perform complex operations such as abdominal tumor removal. He also created the 'Five Animal Frolics' (Wu Qin Xi) calisthenics for health maintenance. Hua Tuo's medical skills represent the highest level of Eastern Han medicine.

v.t: Severus campaigns in Britain
v.s: To strengthen the frontier, Emperor Severus led a large army to campaign in Britain, attacking the Picts. Roman forces advanced into the Scottish Highlands, repaired Hadrian's Wall, and built the Antonine Wall. Severus died in Britain in 211.

v.t: Battle of Guandu
v.s: Cao Cao and Yuan Shao clashed at Guandu (present-day Zhongmu, Henan) in a decisive battle. Yuan Shao had 100,000 troops and ample provisions; Cao Cao had only 20,000 and was short of supplies. Cao Cao, following the advice of Xun Yu and Guo Jia, held his position and waited. Then Yuan Shao's adviser Xu You defected to Cao Cao and suggested a surprise attack on Yuan Shao's grain depot at Wuchao. Cao Cao personally led 5,000 elite cavalry to burn the grain, causing panic in Yuan Shao's army. Cao Cao then attacked and destroyed the main Yuan army, killing over 70,000. Yuan Shao fled back north across the Yellow River with only 800 horsemen. The Battle of Guandu was the decisive battle for Cao Cao's unification of the north, and a classic example of a smaller force defeating a larger one in Chinese history.

v.t: Sun Ce is assassinated; Sun Quan succeeds
v.s: While hunting at Dantu Mountain, Sun Ce was fatally wounded by assassins sent by the family of Xu Gong, whom he had killed. He was only 26. On his deathbed, Sun Ce entrusted his younger brother Sun Quan to Zhang Zhao and Zhou Yu, saying: 'In external matters, consult Zhou Yu; in internal matters, consult Zhang Zhao.' Sun Quan succeeded, and under the guidance of Zhang Zhao and Zhou Yu, stabilized Jiangdong, gradually developing into a power that could rival Cao Cao and Liu Bei.

v.t: Caracalla issues the Constitutio Antoniniana
v.s: Roman Emperor Caracalla (son of Severus) issued the Constitutio Antoniniana, granting Roman citizenship to all free men within the empire. Ostensibly a move to expand citizenship, its real purpose was to increase tax revenue (citizens were subject to inheritance tax and manumission tax). Although it unified legal status, it also devalued citizenship, and the Roman army increasingly came to be composed of barbarians.

v.t: Rabbi Judah HaNasi compiles the Mishnah
v.s: The Jewish rabbi Judah HaNasi began compiling the Mishnah, the first written codification of the Oral Torah. The Mishnah, in six orders (Zera'im, Mo'ed, Nashim, Nezikin, Kodashim, Toharot), covers agriculture, festivals, marriage, civil law, Temple rituals, and purity. It became the core of the Talmud and, second only to the Torah, the most important Jewish scripture, playing a crucial role in preserving Jewish identity during the long diaspora.

v.t: Guan Yu leaves behind his seal and salary to journey a thousand li to find his sworn brother Liu Bei
v.s: Cao Cao attacked Xuzhou; Liu Bei fled to Yuan Shao, while Guan Yu was surrounded at Xiapi and forced to surrender to Cao Cao. Cao Cao treated Guan Yu with great honor, appointing him a lieutenant general. But Guan Yu never forgot his oath to Liu Bei. After repaying Cao Cao by killing the enemy general Yan Liang (Yuan Shao's force), he left behind his official seal and salary, and escorted the two wives of Liu Bei on a thousand-li journey to reunite with him. He passed five passes and killed six generals, finally rejoining Liu Bei and Zhang Fei. Guan Yu's loyalty and righteousness earned him the posthumous title 'Sage of War' (Wu Sheng).
